decisions.title: Define a minimal local EventContext type for Cloudflare Pages handler instead of importing from @cloudflare/workers-types
decisions.summary: The PR fixes a type incompatibility issue tracked in #833. The fix defines a minimal, compatible EventContext on the Hono side rather than importing it from @cloudflare/workers-types.

context.claim: The PR fixes a type incompatibility issue tracked in #833.
context.quote: Fixes #833
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: Using EventContext imported from @cloudflare/workers-types produced a type error.
context.quote: Using `EventContext` imported from `@cloudflare/workers-types` as you did, I got an error, so I solved it by defining a minimal and compatible EventContext on Hono side.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The oldest Cloudflare compatibility date that triggers the error is 2022-01-31, which coincides with a switch from readonly properties to getter/setter accessors in the Workers type declarations.
context.quote: FWIW, the oldest compatibility date that causes the error is 2022-01-31, but I haven't yet figured out which part of the change is the reason.
context.author: yudai-nkt

decision.claim: The fix defines a minimal, compatible EventContext on the Hono side rather than importing it from @cloudflare/workers-types.
decision.quote: I solved it by defining a minimal and compatible EventContext on Hono side. I think the way I did looks good
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The final accepted commit omits passThroughOnException from the locally-defined EventContext so that CI passes.
decision.quote: do not include `passThroughOnException: () => void` in `EventContext`
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The maintainer merged the PR after waiting for but not receiving further feedback from stefanmaric.
decision.quote: OK, no response from stefan, but I'll merge it.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The fix must work across both newer and older Cloudflare compatibility dates.
forces.quote: it works with both newer compatibility date and old one!
forces.author: yudai-nkt

forces.claim: Using a triple-slash reference pollutes Request and other globals, breaking CI.
forces.quote: Ah, CI failed. Looks like triple-slash is polluting the `Request` and other APIs.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The Request used in HonoRequest.raw becomes polluted by the triple-slash directive.
forces.quote: So, the `Request` used in `HonoRequest.raw` is polluted.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Importing EventContext from @cloudflare/workers-types causes a Request type mismatch.
forces.quote: I think triple-slash is appropriate in this case since using `import` will cause a mismatch in the Request type.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: With the locally-defined EventContext, the type resolves to `any` on the pages function side rather than the real EventContext type.
consequences.quote: With my implementation writing `EventContext` in Hono side, that will be `any` in pages functions
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The local EventContext omits passThroughOnException to avoid the CI failure triggered when it was present.
consequences.quote: The `EventContext` did not contain `passThroughOnException` and we needed to remove it, and it has the type.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The team accepts that this is a temporary workaround to be revisited if further issues arise.
consequences.quote: we can revisit this if another problem occurs in the future. Let's go this way for the time being.
consequences.author: yudai-nkt

alternatives.name: Import EventContext directly from @cloudflare/workers-types
alternatives.rejection_reason: Using the imported EventContext produced a type error, and the import causes a mismatch in the Request type.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Using `EventContext` imported from `@cloudflare/workers-types` as you did, I got an error, so I solved it by defining a minimal and compatible EventContext on Hono side.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Use a triple-slash reference directive (/// <reference types="@cloudflare/workers-types" />) instead of importing
alternatives.rejection_reason: Although this preserves the correct EventContext type in pages functions, the triple-slash directive polluted Request and other global APIs, causing CI to fail.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ah, CI failed. Looks like triple-slash is polluting the `Request` and other APIs.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Handle HEAD method automatically via app.get() handler
decisions.summary: The framework previously required users to define a separate app.head() handler to respond to HEAD requests HEAD requests to /foo will be dispatched to app.get('/foo') and the response body will be stripped

context.claim: Semantically, HEAD is defined as the response of GET without the body
context.quote: the HEAD method is essentially "the response of GET without the body".
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The PR resolves issue #1130 which requested HEAD handling
context.quote: This PR allows `app.get()` to handle the HEAD method, resolving issue #1130.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The framework previously required users to define a separate app.head() handler to respond to HEAD requests
context.quote: Currently, we have to define the process in `app.head()` if a HEAD method request comes in.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: yusukebe concluded to merge this PR (core implementation) rather than the middleware alternative
decision.quote: I've realized your implementation is excellent and this PR is better than the middleware pattern.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The final implementation invokes dispatch() without changing request.method for HEAD, incorporating PR #1152
decision.quote: Invoke dispatch() without changing request.method for HEAD method. (#1152)
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: HEAD requests to /foo will be dispatched to app.get('/foo') and the response body will be stripped
decision.quote: Therefore, `HEAD /foo` will be handled by `app.get('/foo')` and return the response without the body.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The maintainer wanted to avoid complicating hono-base.ts core code
forces.quote: We wouldn't have to write more code in the core, such as in `hono-base.ts`. I prefer not to complicate the core.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: In most use cases, the HEAD response is simply the GET response minus the body
forces.quote: I believe in most use cases, the result of a HEAD request will be "the result of a GET request minus the body."
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Users may want to optimize HEAD requests by avoiding expensive body computation when content length is known
forces.quote: sometimes users may be able to optimize requests in interesting ways by avoiding things like computing the body if the body length is known ahead of time
forces.author: ThatOneBro

forces.claim: dispatch() is a private method so increasing its arguments is acceptable
forces.quote: `dispatch` is a private method, so the caller is in a controllable location, and I think the argument increase here is acceptable
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Using an options object for dispatch would add per-call allocation overhead and be hard to mangle
forces.quote:
That there is an overhead if the caller needs to create an object of options at the time of the call.
* That there is a small overhead in `options?.eventOrExecutionCtx`.
* Option object (`DispatchOptions` ) is hard to mangle.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Most web servers enable HEAD handling by default, and web developers typically don't know to enable it manually
forces.quote: most web servers have this feature enabled by default. Most web devs don't know about this feature, and there are larger changes of web devs not enabling them via middleware.
forces.author: renhiyama

consequences.claim: app.head is marked deprecated with intent to remove in a future minor version
consequences.quote: show `depracated` message (will be removed next minor? version)
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users who want to optimize HEAD separately can branch on c.req.method === 'HEAD' inside their GET handler
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Users will no longer be able to define app.head() for HEAD handling (app.head is deprecated)
consequences.quote: Users will not be able to define `app.head()`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Despite being a feature change, the author considered it acceptable to release as a patch because it corrects HEAD handling
consequences.quote: While this is a feature change, on the other hand, it simply corrects the handling of HEAD. Therefore, I believe it's acceptable to release it as a patch.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Implement HEAD handling as opt-in middleware (PR #1143)
alternatives.rejection_reason: usualoma argued HEAD-as-GET-minus-body is standard web server behavior and hono-base.ts is not complicated enough to justify pushing it out to middleware; yusukebe agreed after seeing the final implementation.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I am not against using middleware, but I still think this PR is better. I think it is standard web server behavior, and hono-base.ts is not that complicated.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Keep app.head() as an override mechanism (do not deprecate)
alternatives.rejection_reason: yusukebe said supporting app.head() as an override above app.get() would be difficult to implement and make the code very complex.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I somewhat agree, but it's difficult to implement. If we were to do so, the code would become very complex.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Pass dispatch options as an options object (DispatchOptions)
alternatives.rejection_reason: An options-object form was rejected due to per-call allocation overhead, overhead of optional property access, and that option objects are hard to mangle; instead dispatch() arguments were made non-omittable positional params.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
That there is an overhead if the caller needs to create an object of options at the time of the call.
* That there is a small overhead in `options?.eventOrExecutionCtx`.
* Option object (`DispatchOptions` ) is hard to mangle.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Implement css helper using Promise/callback mechanism (not jsxRenderer-dependent)
decisions.summary: The css helper uses the callback mechanism already used by Suspense and ErrorBoundary to insert the class name. The implementation was approved and merged into the feat/css-helper branch (#1850).

context.claim: The css helper uses the callback mechanism already used by Suspense and ErrorBoundary to insert the class name.
context.quote: The callback mechanism used in Suspense and ErrorBoundary is used to insert the class name.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: The helper is primarily intended for JSX components but also works in html tag functions, and does not depend on jsxRenderer.
context.quote:
It is primarily targeted to be used in JSX components, but can also be used in `html` tag functions.
It does not depend on jsxRenderer.
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The implementation was approved and merged into the feat/css-helper branch (#1850).
decision.quote: Okay! I'll merge this into #1850
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The core approach of using Promise and callback was accepted as the only viable way.
decision.quote: Using `Promise` and callback is the only way I can think of to do it, so that's fine.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: A cx() helper compatible with @emotion/css was added to combine multiple css classes, including support for external class names.
decision.quote:
feat(css): add cx() helper

`cx()` is a helper function to combine multiple css classes.
This function is compatible with `cx()` in @emotion/css.
decision.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Implementation must not depend on jsxRenderer so users can use any rendering pattern.
forces.quote: It does not depend on jsxRenderer, so you can use any pattern you like.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Performance when class names are predefined globally is a requirement — CSS should not be recalculated per request.
forces.quote: In environments where initialization does not occur with every request, It works very fast because CSS is not recalculated when class names are predefined globally.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The feature needs to support pseudo-elements, pseudo-selectors, and @keyframes.
forces.quote: I think we have to implement pseudo-elements, pseudo-selectors, and `@keyframes`.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Variables embedded in ${} are escaped to match the existing Hono html tag behavior.
forces.quote:
Variables embedded in `${}` are now escaped. This is the same as html`

${'(><;) crying...

` being escaped, which is a satisfactory specification in the hono.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Hono is often used with external CSS, so mixing external class names must be supported.
forces.quote: I personally would like to see a "Using class names from other sources" feature. This is because, in the case of Hono, we sometimes use it with external CSS.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The mechanism is complicated because the string is always a Promise<string> requiring a callback to obtain the final result.
consequences.quote: The mechanism is complicated because the string is always a `Promise<string>` and callback must be properly called to obtain the final result.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Allowing Promise<string> in the class attribute may confuse some linters.
consequences.quote: Allowing Promise<string> in the `class` attribute may confuse some linters (e.g. https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/1812 ).
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Current implementation is considered MVP quality; memory/minify efficiency improvements are deferred until the feature is validated.
consequences.quote: The current implementation could be better, so I'm sure there are more efficient ways to use memory and minify. However, these improvements can only be made after we decide this feature is worthwhile. We believe the current implementation is of sufficient quality to be tested as an MVP.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Conditional class names and using external class names via cx() were deferred as not implemented in this PR.
consequences.quote:
The following features of @emotion/css have not been implemented, but I believe they meet the current requirements.

* Conditional class names
* Using class names from other sources
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Complexity of Promise<string>/callback is hidden from users who use c.html().
consequences.quote: However, if you use c.html(), you do not need to be aware of this complexity.
consequences.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Array-of-classes syntax (class={[classTitle, classH1]}) that concatenates into space-separated class names
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would re-expose users to CSS cascading/selector hell, which JSS in CSS aims to eliminate; array ordering also differs from CSS semantics where class order is irrelevant.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: We could consider an implementation that would result in the following, but we don't think this would accomplish one of the things I wanted to solve with JSS in CSS: freedom from the hell of CSS cascading and selctor.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Emotion-style class composition by embedding one class inside another (const myPrimaryButton = css`${btn} background: blue;`)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered more explicit but less ergonomic than cx(); yusukebe preferred a dedicated cx() helper so users become explicitly aware of ordering.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: If we create a function `cx` in hono/css, users will be explicitly aware of the order by using it.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Embed sub css feature (programmatic generation of css via nested css`` templates)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author suggested it might be overkill and offered to revert; ultimately retained but discussed as potentially removable.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Nevertheless, it may be overkill. I think we can revert this commit if it is not needed.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Using Map instead of WeakMap for context/usedClassName storage
alternatives.rejection_reason: Switched to WeakMap to avoid memory leaks.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: feat(css): use WeakMap instead of Map, to avoid memory leak
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Escaping all slashes in embedded CSS values
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would prevent use of url() without escaping; narrowed to escaping only close tags '</...>'.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: feat(css): escape only close tag "</...>", not all slashes. we can use url() without escaping.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Support '&' pseudo-element selectors via helper-side implementation
alternatives.rejection_reason: Implementation was overly complex, and native CSS nesting is now almost universally supported by browsers, so it was removed in favor of relying on native CSS nesting.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Bad News: The selector for pseudo-elements using '&' has been removed due to its overly complex implementation.
* Good News: Pseudo-elements using '&' are now almost universally supported by browsers.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Introduce WebSocket Helper / Adapter with per-runtime adapters exporting `upgradeWebSocket`
decisions.summary: The PR implements a WebSocket helper so that the WebSocket API can be used through Hono more easily, addressing a long-standing feature request referenced as issue #1153. Ship a WebSocket helper with per-runtime adapters (Bun, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Cloudflare Pages), exposing `upgradeWebSocket` from each runtime's adapter module.

context.claim: The PR implements a WebSocket helper so that the WebSocket API can be used through Hono more easily, addressing a long-standing feature request referenced as issue #1153.
context.quote:
Reference: https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/1153

If we marge this PR, we can use WebSocket API through Hono easier.
context.author: nakasyou

context.claim: WebSocket implementation differs significantly across runtimes (Bun, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, etc.), so a per-runtime adapter approach is needed.
context.quote: its implementation varies widely from runtime to runtime, so I think the current implementation where `hono/ws` exports only type `UpgradeWebSocket` is fine.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Ship a WebSocket helper with per-runtime adapters (Bun, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Cloudflare Pages), exposing `upgradeWebSocket` from each runtime's adapter module.
decision.quote:
### Implemented adapters:
- [x] Bun
- [x] Cloudflare Workers
- [ ] Node.js
- [ ] AWS lambda
- [x] Cloudflare Pages
- [x] Deno
decision.author: nakasyou

decision.claim: Consolidate imports so users import `upgradeWebSocket` from the runtime adapter (e.g. `hono/cloudflare-workers`), and remove the alternative `hono/ws/{adapter}` import paths.
decision.quote: Hi @yusukebe, I removed how you can import from ws adapter such as `hono/ws/cloudflare-workers`.
decision.author: nakasyou

decision.claim: yusukebe approved the PR as the final sign-off.
decision.quote: Thanks! Finished! Let's land it.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Hono's policy is to avoid depending on external libraries, which constrains how WebSocket support can be implemented.
forces.quote: Because our policy is "not to depend on external libraries".
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Shipping quickly was prioritized over a longer design discussion.
forces.quote: But the feature is already in place and ready to ship as soon as this PR review is done. For us, it is important to "release quickly.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: A good adapter API should let users switch runtimes by simply changing the import source.
forces.quote: in the case of an adapter, I think a good API is one that allows you to simply change the import source when the runtime is changed.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Cloudflare Workers does not fire the `open` event for WebSockets, which constrains the API surface.
forces.quote: That's right, `open` doesn't work on Cloudflare. It didn't work in the production environment either.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The client-side `$ws` implementation is acknowledged as not ideal and the message data path is not type-safe (deferred).
consequences.quote:
Cons for client:
 - This implementing code is not smart, I think good solution it is exists.
 - Not type safe
consequences.author: nakasyou

consequences.claim: Typed message validation for WebSocket data is deferred to a future PR rather than implemented now.
consequences.quote: But, we can implement it in future, not in this PR.
consequences.author: nakasyou

consequences.claim: Node.js, AWS Lambda, lambda-edge, and Netlify adapters are not implemented in this PR; Vercel is excluded because it doesn't support WebSockets.
consequences.quote:
- [ ] Node.js
- [ ] AWS lambda
- [x] Cloudflare Pages
- [x] Deno
- [ ] lambda-edge
- [ ] netlify
- [ ] ~~Vercel~~ (Vercel doesn't support WebSockets)
consequences.author: nakasyou

consequences.claim: The `upgradeWebSocket` callback-creation API may be less performant than Bun's recommended approach, but was accepted.
consequences.quote: Mind you, also the current API design in this PR that makes new callbacks inside `upgradeWebSocket` is not most performant choice.
consequences.author: pi0

consequences.claim: Migration to CrossWS remains possible in the future but is not pursued now.
consequences.quote: We cannot collaborate immediately, but we may be able to change the logic of WebSocket to CrossWS in the future.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Import `upgradeWebSocket` from a runtime-neutral `hono/ws` module that takes a per-runtime context (e.g. `upgradeWebSocket(cfWebSocketContext, ...)`)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would only make sense if `hono/ws` had significant common processing, but WebSocket implementation varies widely per runtime, so a runtime-specific adapter that the user selects via import is preferred.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: In the case of your proposed API, it makes sense if the `upgradeWebSocket` exported from `'hono/ws'` has a lot of common processes. However, its implementation varies widely from runtime to runtime, so I think the current implementation where `hono/ws` exports only type `UpgradeWebSocket` is fine.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Add typed message validators / typed `send` and `onmessage` for WebSocket in this PR
alternatives.rejection_reason: Too complex to support on both server and client `send`/`onmessage`; the author and maintainer agreed to defer it rather than complicate the type definitions now.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ah, I see. But maybe it's super difficult! Both server and client `send` and `onmessage` must be supported. I don't want to make the type definitions too complicated, so I think it is unnecessary to implement this in this PR.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Ship a dedicated Cloudflare Pages WebSocket adapter
alternatives.rejection_reason: Not needed because the Cloudflare Workers adapter already works in Pages.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: And, I discovered that Workers Adapter is working in Pages, so we don't need Pages adapter for WebSocket.
alternatives.author: nakasyou

alternatives.name: Add `bun-types` as a devDependency to build the Bun adapter
alternatives.rejection_reason: Removed; the adapter was implemented without requiring the `bun-types` package.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
### ~~New `devDependencies`~~
- ~~[`bun-types`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bun-types)~~
alternatives.author: nakasyou

alternatives.name: Embed UnJS CrossWS as the cross-runtime WebSocket layer
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would require a longer design discussion and conflicts with Hono's policy of not depending on external libraries; the team prioritized shipping quickly.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: That is certainly true. But the feature is already in place and ready to ship as soon as this PR review is done. For us, it is important to "release quickly. Instead, a long discussion is needed on the use of CrossWS. Because our policy is "not to depend on external libraries".
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Expose `upgradeWebSocket` from sub-path imports like `hono/ws/cloudflare-workers`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Redundant with exporting `upgradeWebSocket` directly from the existing runtime adapter module (`hono/cloudflare-workers`), matching the `serveStatic` / `toSSG` pattern; the sub-paths were removed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
This approach is the same as `serveStatic`, as you say. 

So, I think the following `. /ws/cloudflare-workers` etc. are not necessary?
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Name the Bun factory function `createWebSocket` (without the `Bun` prefix)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer reconsidered and preferred `createBunWebSocket` because the function returns Bun-specific objects, making the Bun-specific name clearer.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I'm reconsidering. `createBunWebSocket` might be a good name for this since it contains Bun specific objects.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Convert CSSProperties from type alias to interface to allow declaration merging in JSX
decisions.summary: Type aliases in TypeScript cannot be merged, so the documented pattern of overriding type definitions does not work for CSSProperties. The maintainer accepted the change, fixed formatting themselves, and merged the PR.

context.claim: Type aliases in TypeScript cannot be merged, so the documented pattern of overriding type definitions does not work for CSSProperties.
context.quote: Type declarations don't allow to merge, so [overriding type declaration](https://hono.dev/docs/guides/jsx#override-type-definitions) from docs doesn't work for CSSProperties.
context.author: jonasnobile

decision.claim: The maintainer accepted the change, fixed formatting themselves, and merged the PR.
decision.quote: I've fixed the format myself. I'll merge this now. Thank you for your contribution!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The maintainer characterized the change as a good improvement.
decision.quote: Nice! This is a good improvement.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The documented override-type-definitions guide requires that CSSProperties support declaration merging.
forces.quote: [overriding type declaration](https://hono.dev/docs/guides/jsx#override-type-definitions) from docs doesn't work for CSSProperties.
forces.author: jonasnobile

consequences.claim: The style prop continues to accept string values because the underlying `{}` type inherently permits that; the change only improves typing when styles are written as an object.
consequences.quote: CSS properties could always be written as strings since `{}` inherently allows that. The change simply improves the typing when you choose to write styles as an object.
consequences.author: jonasnobile

decisions.title: Replace type intersection with union in Hono's route method to reduce type instantiation cost
decisions.summary: Massive intersection usage in Hono's types was causing high type instantiation counts during TypeScript compilation. The team decided to replace the intersection in the `route` method with a union rather than replacing all intersections throughout the type system.

context.claim: Massive intersection usage in Hono's types was causing high type instantiation counts during TypeScript compilation.
context.quote: I found that massive amount of intersection increase type instantiation.
context.author: m-shaka

context.claim: Type performance is considered a top priority concern for the Hono maintainers.
context.quote: Plus, regarding the types, we must care about the performance, most importantly.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The team decided to replace the intersection in the `route` method with a union rather than replacing all intersections throughout the type system.
decision.quote: I replaced intersection in `route` method with union.
decision.author: m-shaka

decision.claim: The maintainer confirmed the approach and approved introducing the change.
decision.quote: This is awesome! I also tried it; I could feel it became faster with `app.route()`. Let's introduce this.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Author gave the final green light to merge.
decision.quote: Go!
decision.author: m-shaka

forces.claim: Apps added via `route` are independent of each other, making union semantically appropriate there.
forces.quote: apps added through `route` are independent each other, I suppose
forces.author: m-shaka

forces.claim: Handler type customization by users is a low-priority concern since only a few users do it, and the team prioritizes runtime behavior preservation over handler type stability.
forces.quote: We can care less about changing the type definitions of handlers. A few users customize them, but we don't have to care about them though we have to take care of actual runtime behaviors.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: A follow-up PR will be needed to improve the performance measurement display to cover both patterns (with and without `app.route()`).
consequences.quote: After merging it and this PR, I'll improve the display of the result
consequences.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: `ExcludeEmptyObject` was relocated to `utils/type.ts` as part of the refactor.
consequences.quote: refactor: move ExcludeEmptyObject to utils/type.ts
consequences.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: Replacing intersection with union in the `route` method reduced type instantiations by about 60% in the test scenario.
consequences.quote: Instantiations    1506985    3691507
consequences.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: `Instantiations` is down 60% compared to before the change.
consequences.quote: `Instantiaions` is down 60%
consequences.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: This change may break downstream tools that rely on Hono's schema type shape, as reported by a user whose OpenAPI generator broke post-release.
consequences.quote: However, this change of the schema types has broken my generator.
consequences.author: miyaji255

alternatives.name: Measure performance only for the `app.route()` pattern (single-pattern benchmark)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer preferred measuring both with- and without-`app.route()` patterns because real user codebases include both, though this was deferred to a later PR rather than rejected outright.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: We can choose only the latter to measure it, but in my opinion, it's better to know both performances because there are cases if a user uses `app.route()` or not.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Replace all intersections with unions throughout Hono's types (the original, broader approach)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Using union everywhere broke method chaining: after chaining `.get('/bar/:id', ...)` followed by `.post(...)`, `c.req.param('id')` became `string | undefined` instead of the expected narrow type. The author judged there were likely more such pitfalls.
alternatives.author: m-shaka

alternatives.name: Keep existing intersection-based types (do nothing)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Type performance is a top priority, and the measured reduction in instantiation cost was too significant to forgo.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Plus, regarding the types, we must care about the performance, most importantly.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Use `#` private class fields instead of TypeScript `private` keyword to reduce minified bundle size
decisions.summary: The TypeScript `private` keyword does not allow esbuild to mangle private method names, leaving them in the minified output Private methods in the core (including Context and routers) are converted from the `private` keyword to the `#` ECMAScript private syntax

context.claim: The TypeScript `private` keyword does not allow esbuild to mangle private method names, leaving them in the minified output
context.quote: If you use the `private` keyword for the private method, the function name is not minifed with esbuild
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The 'Hello World' app with the `hono/tiny` preset measured 12167 bytes before the change and 11688 bytes after, a reduction of 479 bytes when minified with esbuild
context.quote: `479 bytes` will be reduced without changing any logic and without performance degradation!
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The decision was finalized by going with usualoma's compromise proposal before merge
decision.quote: Sorry to bother you. Let's go as you suggested. Can you review it?
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Private methods in the core (including Context and routers) are converted from the `private` keyword to the `#` ECMAScript private syntax
decision.quote: This PR reduces the bundle size using `#` for the private methods instead of the `private` keyword when the code is minified.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The change was extended to routers based on reviewer suggestion
decision.quote: Good idea! Updated https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3596/commits/24d4fb157bdbdce0d38717e9ef4d55446f54dcea
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Reducing bundle size may decrease parsing time and speed up deploy uploads to production
forces.quote: parsing time may be reduced, and the file upload time to the production environment will be faster, although super slightly.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Instance methods defined by assignment (e.g. `#method = this.otherMethod`) have measurable overhead at instance-creation time compared to methods defined directly
forces.quote: instance methods are faster to create instances if they are written directly than if they are defined by assignment.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Benchmarks showed `class B` (method declaration style) is up to ~1500x faster on Node and ~700x faster on Bun than `class A` (assignment style) for instance creation
forces.quote:
class B
   1498.52x faster than class A
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: `c.#newResponse` is called frequently internally while `c.newResponse` is seldom called by user apps, so the hot path should be prioritized
forces.quote: `c.newResponse` is not called in many apps. On the other hand, `c.#newResponse` is often called multiple times internally. Therefore, I think it would be better to prioritize improving the performance of `c.#newResponse`.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The public `newResponse` API requires preserving its overloaded type signatures (status code vs. ResponseInit)
forces.quote: I think that would mean that the intended type would no longer be available for the public API `newResponse()`.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Minified bundle size decreases by 479 bytes for the hello-world `hono/tiny` case with no logic change and no performance degradation
consequences.quote: `479 bytes` will be reduced without changing any logic and without performance degradation!
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: `#newResponse` becomes the method-style implementation and public `newResponse` is defined as a small wrapper, so the internal hot path avoids assignment-style overhead
consequences.quote: why not give `c.#newResponse` priority with the following changes?
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: TypeScript transpiles `private` to public at the JS level, which is why esbuild cannot safely mangle those names; `#` is a real runtime-private feature and therefore safely manglable
consequences.quote: TypeScript makes all properties with private modifiers public properties when transpiling them to JavaScript.
consequences.author: EdamAme-x

alternatives.name: Keep `newResponse` as the primary implementation and have `#newResponse` wrap it
alternatives.rejection_reason: `#newResponse` is the hot path called internally many times while public `newResponse` is rarely called by apps, so the roles should be swapped to prioritize internal performance.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: `c.newResponse` is not called in many apps. On the other hand, `c.#newResponse` is often called multiple times internally. Therefore, I think it would be better to prioritize improving the performance of `c.#newResponse`.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Collapse the overloaded `NewResponse` interface into a single union-argument signature to share one type between private and public methods
alternatives.rejection_reason: The single-signature form would accept invalid argument combinations that the original overloads intentionally disallowed, weakening the public API's types.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I don't think that's what we wanted to do with the original definition, because it would accept the following.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Remove the `NewResponse`/`BodyRespond` interfaces entirely and redeclare overloads directly on both `newResponse` and `body`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered as a 'one step further' performance-and-type-correctness option, but rejected in favor of the smaller compromise that doesn't require major changes to existing code.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think the proposal described in the following comments is a good compromise that does not involve major changes to the current code and is unlikely to cause a drop in performance.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Keep the TypeScript `private` keyword
alternatives.rejection_reason: With `private`, esbuild does not minify the private method names, leaving the full name in the minified output and wasting bytes.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: If you use the `private` keyword for the private method, the function name is not minifed with esbuild
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Define `#newResponse` via assignment syntax (`#newResponse: NewResponse = (...) => {...}`)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Assignment-style method definitions have non-trivial per-instance creation overhead (benchmarks show up to ~1500x slower on Node) compared to method-declaration style.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: the `#method = this.otherMethod` pattern has a non-trivial overhead even in deno.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Use crypto.subtle with chained per-chunk hashing to generate ETags from ReadableStream bodies
decisions.summary: The etag middleware was generating the same weak etag for different body contents when responses were streamed (e.g., ~20KB responses on Cloudflare Workers). Generate the etag digest by, for each chunk, calling crypto.subtle.digest over the previous hash concatenated with the new chunk, producing a chained hash across all chunks.

context.claim: When the response body is a ReadableStream, the existing sha1 call on the stream was not producing content-dependent hashes.
context.quote:
const hash = await sha1(res.clone().body || '')
      etag = weak ? `W/"${hash}"` : `"${hash}"`
context.author: markusahlstrand

context.claim: The etag middleware was generating the same weak etag for different body contents when responses were streamed (e.g., ~20KB responses on Cloudflare Workers).
context.quote: Generate a large response (20kb) with the etag middleware using weak etags, change a character in the response again.
context.author: markusahlstrand

context.claim: The PR fixes issue #3536.
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The implementation uses only the web-standard crypto.subtle API.
decision.quote: It uses only the web standard `crypto.subtle`, and is characterized by its low memory load even for large-sized files.
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The maintainer approved and merged the PR.
decision.quote: Looks good to me! I'll merge this. Thanks!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Generate the etag digest by, for each chunk, calling crypto.subtle.digest over the previous hash concatenated with the new chunk, producing a chained hash across all chunks.
decision.quote: There are several ways to generate digests from ReadableStream, but here we have used a method that includes the previous hash value for each read, so that unique digests are generated for the same continuous data.
decision.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Digests must be unique for continuous data even when the body is delivered as a stream.
forces.quote: we have used a method that includes the previous hash value for each read, so that unique digests are generated for the same continuous data.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The solution must have low memory usage even for large files.
forces.quote: is characterized by its low memory load even for large-sized files.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: An exact SHA1 of the whole body is not required for etag purposes.
forces.quote: an exact sha1 is not necessary for etags
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The approach should prefer web-standard APIs over node-specific APIs when web-standard suffices.
forces.quote: I didn’t adopt it because I thought that ‘node:*’ should not be used where `crypto.subtle` is sufficient.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: If the same overall data is split into a different number of chunks, the produced digest will differ; this is accepted because it is unlikely in practice on the same server architecture.
consequences.quote: Even if the overall data is the same, if the number of chunks read is different, a different digest will be generated, but since it is unlikely that the same architecture (server) will have different data divisions, I think it will work effectively in practice.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Tests for empty stream and null body were added at the maintainer's request.
consequences.quote: test(middleware/etag): add tests for empty stream and null body
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Tightening the Data type (replacing `object` with `JSONValue`) was deferred to a separate PR due to potential compatibility impact.
consequences.quote: However, there is a slight possibility (I think it's very slight) that it could affect compatibility, so I think it would be better to consider it in a separate pull request.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: ReadableStream support was removed from the crypto util's Data type as part of this change.
consequences.quote: refactor(utils/crypto): remove ReadableStream support
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: crypto.subtle is substantially faster than a self-implemented incremental SHA1 (3.55x on Node, 10.69x on Bun, 3.26x on Deno in the author's benchmark).
consequences.quote:
crypto.subtle
   3.55x faster than IncrementalSha1
consequences.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Self-implemented incremental SHA1 generator in TypeScript
alternatives.rejection_reason: Produces an exact SHA1 across the whole stream, but exact SHA1 isn't required for etags and benchmarks show it is much slower than crypto.subtle (3.26x–10.69x slower across Node/Bun/Deno).
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: However, I did not adopt this because an exact sha1 is not necessary for etags, and it is also slow.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Use node:crypto for incremental hashing
alternatives.rejection_reason: Although node:crypto works across runtimes and supports incremental hashing, the author prefers not to use node:* APIs when the web-standard crypto.subtle is sufficient.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: However, I didn’t adopt it because I thought that ‘node:*’ should not be used where `crypto.subtle` is sufficient.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Calculate Context<Env> type for each handler in middleware chain
decisions.summary: In middleware chains with more than two middlewares, intermediate middlewares lose the custom context type and revert to BlankEnv. All Context<Env> types in handlers will be recalculated so that each handler respects type changes made by previous handlers.

context.claim: In middleware chains with more than two middlewares, intermediate middlewares lose the custom context type and revert to BlankEnv.
context.quote: In a chain with more than two middlewares, intermediate middlewares lose the custom context type and revert to `BlankEnv`, while the last middleware maintains the correct type.
context.author: Ariel-Moroshko

context.claim: Typing the Hono instance itself with new Hono<UserContext>() works around the issue but introduces a type safety problem because variables become accessible in middlewares before they are actually set.
context.quote: While this approach fixes the type inference, it introduces a type safety issue. The variables become accessible in all middlewares, even before they are actually set.
context.author: Ariel-Moroshko

context.claim: The maintainer confirmed the behavior is a bug or unimplemented feature.
context.quote: Thank you for the issue. This is a bug. Or it has not been implemented well.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: All Context<Env> types in handlers will be recalculated so that each handler respects type changes made by previous handlers.
decision.quote: This PR makes all `Context<Env>` in handlers respect type changes made by previous handlers.
decision.author: sushichan044

decision.claim: Maintainer decided to merge despite the performance cost, reasoning that correct type inference is important enough to accept the degradation and that follow-up optimizations can mitigate it.
decision.quote: It introduces a performance decrease for type inferences, but it is also important that the type inference works as expected correctly. Fortunately, the tiny changes #3836 and #3837 can improve the TypeScript performance. So, first, merge this PR and improve the types with some techniques
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: TypeScript performance is critical to developer experience, creating tension with a fix that increases type-check cost.
forces.quote: TypeScript performance is critical to DX, so I am considering whether or not to allow performance degradation due to this change.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Correctness of type inference across the middleware chain is an overriding requirement.
forces.quote: it is also important that the type inference works as expected correctly
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Any fix must avoid exposing context variables in handlers before they are set, which rules out typing the whole Hono instance.
forces.quote: The variables become accessible in all middlewares, even before they are actually set. For example, if userId is only set in the second middleware, the type system would incorrectly allow access to it in the first middleware where it hasn't been initialized yet.
forces.author: Ariel-Moroshko

consequences.claim: Type-check time increased from 2.31s to 2.52s and total compile time from 2.98s to 3.19s in the author's benchmark, which the author judged acceptable.
consequences.quote:
Check time:       2.31s      2.52s
Emit time:        0.00s      0.00s
Total time:       2.98s      3.19s
consequences.author: sushichan044

consequences.claim: The author judged the increase in type calculation cost acceptable.
consequences.quote: I think this is an acceptable increase in type calculation cost.
consequences.author: sushichan044

consequences.claim: Memory use for type checking rose from 271,995K to 317,189K, and instantiations from 492,334 to 548,281.
consequences.quote:
Instantiations:  492334     548281
Memory used:    271995K    317189K
consequences.author: sushichan044

consequences.claim: Follow-up PRs #3836 and #3837 are expected to claw back some of the TypeScript performance loss.
consequences.quote: Fortunately, the tiny changes #3836 and #3837 can improve the TypeScript performance.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Typing the Hono instance itself with new Hono<UserContext>()
alternatives.rejection_reason: It fixes inference but breaks type safety by making context variables accessible in middlewares before they are actually set.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: While this approach fixes the type inference, it introduces a type safety issue. The variables become accessible in all middlewares, even before they are actually set.
alternatives.author: Ariel-Moroshko

alternatives.name: Minimal patch changing only the E3 default to IntersectNonAnyTypes<[E, E2]> in HandlerInterface
alternatives.rejection_reason: Proposed by the maintainer as a small fix and confirmed to work by another maintainer, but superseded by this PR's broader approach that recalculates Context<Env> for every handler in the chain.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
-    E3 extends Env = E,
+    E3 extends Env = IntersectNonAnyTypes<[E, E2]>,
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Restrict c.json/c.text/c.html to ContentfulStatusCode at the type level
decisions.summary: Calling c.json() with status 204 throws a runtime TypeError because Response cannot have a body with that status c.json(), c.text(), and c.html() are prohibited at the type level from being called with 101, 204, 205, and 304

context.claim: Calling c.json() with status 204 throws a runtime TypeError because Response cannot have a body with that status
context.quote: TypeError: Response constructor: Invalid response status code 204
context.author: PlayerNguyen

context.claim: The author personally hit this issue in production after it slipped through a PR review
context.quote: just encountered this. While it's an easy fix, this was missed in one of my PRs and caused a few errors in prod.
context.author: askorupskyy

context.claim: Status 204 requires an empty body per HTTP spec
context.quote: When status code is 204, Response can't contain any content. Body is empty always.
context.author: vanodevium

decision.claim: The maintainer merged the change and decided to ship it in the next patch release
decision.quote: I'll merge this now and include this change in the next patch release since this is `feat` change but it's slight.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: c.json(), c.text(), and c.html() are prohibited at the type level from being called with 101, 204, 205, and 304
decision.quote: All in all, prohibits returning `c.json()` or `c.text()` or `c.html()` with 101, 204, 205, and 304 status codes.
decision.author: askorupskyy

forces.claim: The scope needed to cover all contentless status codes, not just 204
forces.quote: If so, it should support other status codes, not only `204`
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Adding this typing noticeably complicates the Context type definitions
forces.quote: But there is one problem, and it complicates the types somewhat.
forces.author: EdamAme-x

forces.claim: TypeScript should surface a compile-time error instead of letting the runtime throw
forces.quote: This way TypeScript would let us know if we're trying to do something that would cause errors in runtime
forces.author: askorupskyy

consequences.claim: Previously any integer status was accepted, now callers must pass a value typed as ContentfulStatusCode
consequences.quote: Yeah this is a breaking change, specially since an int value was previously acceptable while now we need to have typed num that is valid for `ContentfulStatusCode`.
consequences.author: bombillazo

consequences.claim: Existing code using c.json(null, 204) now fails to type-check, which users perceive as a breaking change shipped in a patch release
consequences.quote: This change is causing all my routes that respond with a `ContentlessStatusCode` (101 | 204 | 205 | 304) to now throw a type error because I am using the `Context.json` function as follows: `return c.json(null, 204);`.
consequences.author: lewisedc

consequences.claim: Users are expected to migrate contentless responses to c.body(null, <code>)
consequences.quote: If you wan to return 204 with no body, you can return `c.body(null, 204)`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Restrict only status code 204 (narrow fix)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected as insufficient — yusukebe pointed out the restriction must cover all contentless status codes, not only 204, so the implementation was broadened to 101/204/205/304.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: If so, it should support other status codes, not only `204`:
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Leave it as a runtime-only error and document c.body(null, 204) as the workaround
alternatives.rejection_reason: This was the prior status quo (the issue had been closed with this workaround), but the author argued it was error-prone in practice — it had slipped past review and caused production errors — so a compile-time guard was preferred.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: While it's an easy fix, this was missed in one of my PRs and caused a few errors in prod. Do you think it could be a good idea to omit the `204` from acceptable response codes in `c.json()` and whatnot?
alternatives.author: askorupskyy

decisions.title: Make ResponseInit generic over StatusCode so c.json/c.text reject contentless status codes in the status property
decisions.summary: After merging #3763, c.json() and c.text() no longer accept a contentless status code as the second argument. Make ResponseInit generic over StatusCode (ResponseInit<T extends StatusCode = StatusCode>) so its status property uses the narrowed StatusCode type.

context.claim: After merging #3763, c.json() and c.text() no longer accept a contentless status code as the second argument.
context.quote: From merging #3763, `c.json()` or `c.text()` can no longer have a contentless status code as the second argument.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: However, when a ResponseInit object is passed as the second argument, a contentless status code in the status property is not rejected — creating an inconsistency.
context.quote: In this PR, if a method such as `c.json()` has `ResponseInit` as its second argument, specifying contentless code for the status property will cause a type error.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Make ResponseInit generic over StatusCode (ResponseInit<T extends StatusCode = StatusCode>) so its status property uses the narrowed StatusCode type.
decision.quote:
interface ResponseInit<T extends StatusCode = StatusCode> {
   headers?: ResponseHeadersInit
-  status?: number
+  status?: T
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Introduce a ResponseOrInit<T> = ResponseInit<T> | Response union because ResponseInit no longer satisfies the Response type.
decision.quote: It has `Response | ResponseInit` because `ResponseInit` no longer satisfies the type of `Response`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Accept the change and merge despite the TS performance concern, treating TypeScript performance optimization as a separate issue.
decision.quote:
Speaking of this change, I think it is necessary to make `ResponseInit` behave in the same way as #3770. So I think it is okay to merge it, ignoring the performance issue.

As for the performance of TypeScript, it is ideal to optimize it as a separate issue.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Final decision to merge was announced after reviewer approvals.
decision.quote: Let's go with this. Merging now.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Consistency: ResponseInit should behave the same as the positional status argument to c.json()/c.text() that was tightened in #3763.
forces.quote: I think it is necessary to make `ResponseInit` behave in the same way as #3770.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: TypeScript compiler performance is a constraint — the change introduces minor performance degradation.
forces.quote: However, it causes minor performance degradation in TypeScript, so we need to be careful about it.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Key TS performance metrics to watch for this kind of change are number of Types and Check Time.
forces.quote: the numbers we have to pay attention to most is `# of Types` and `Check Time`. In this PR the diff for those is minimal so I suggest we merge this.
forces.author: askorupskyy

forces.claim: The existing TS perf benchmark doesn't exercise the second argument of json, so its numbers don't fully represent the impact.
forces.quote: Our test case doesn't use the second argument of `json`, so I reckon it's not a suitable one to check the impact of this change
forces.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: Passing a contentless status code via ResponseInit.status now produces a type error — the intended correct behavior.
consequences.quote: if a method such as `c.json()` has `ResponseInit` as its second argument, specifying contentless code for the status property will cause a type error.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Because ResponseInit is now generic, a new ResponseOrInit<T> union with Response is required where either is accepted.
consequences.quote: It has `Response | ResponseInit` because `ResponseInit` no longer satisfies the type of `Response`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Measured TS compiler impact in an augmented test case was small: Instantiations went from 3,047,508 to 3,048,953 and Check time from 2.97s to 3.04s.
consequences.quote: `Instantiations` increased but just slightly
consequences.author: m-shaka

consequences.claim: TypeScript performance optimization is deferred to a separate issue rather than being solved in this PR.
consequences.quote: As for the performance of TypeScript, it is ideal to optimize it as a separate issue.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Broader caution is warranted about adding more TS types into Hono's Context in the future.
consequences.quote: we have to be careful overall about populating  Hono's context with more TS.
consequences.author: askorupskyy

alternatives.name: Redesign context response methods (c.text()/c.json()) to each return a distinct shape with fields like status, headers, content, instead of extending Context with more generic typing
alternatives.rejection_reason: Raised by a reviewer as a simpler and more scalable long-term direction for Hono's Context typing, but treated as out of scope for this PR; the immediate fix via generic ResponseInit was merged instead and broader Context refactoring was left as a larger future discussion.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think the subject of types is a part of a much greater discussion, but to put it simply I think _less inference overall = better_. I think the Hono Context as a concept makes it hard to make Hono scalable enough for the Typescript compiler. In this particular case, if each context method, such as .text() or .json() were to return a set of properties such as `status`, `headers`, `content`, this would be a much simpler and scalable fix
alternatives.author: askorupskyy

alternatives.name: Defer the change until TypeScript performance impact could be fully optimized
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author decided consistency with #3763 behavior was more important and chose to merge despite the performance concern, treating TS perf as a separate follow-up issue.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
So I think it is okay to merge it, ignoring the performance issue.

As for the performance of TypeScript, it is ideal to optimize it as a separate issue.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Add language detector middleware with multi-source detection and shared Accept header parser
decisions.summary: Hono lacked a standardized way to detect user language preferences across different sources such as URL, cookies, and headers. Ship a new languageDetector middleware imported from hono/language that detects language from query, cookie, header, and path with configurable order.

context.claim: Hono lacked a standardized way to detect user language preferences across different sources such as URL, cookies, and headers.
context.quote: While building an i18n system for Hono, I noticed the lack of a standardized way to detect user language preferences across different sources (URL, cookies, headers, etc.).
context.author: lord007tn

context.claim: Without this, each developer had to implement custom language detection logic per project.
context.quote: Developers need to write custom code for each project
context.author: lord007tn

context.claim: The existing Accepts helper only covered the Accept-Language header, not path/query/cookie fallbacks.
context.quote: accepts only limited to header in my case it is a series of fallbacks that you configure to get the user language
context.author: lord007tn

context.claim: The middleware is intended as a foundation for future i18n functionality in Hono.
context.quote: Add language detector middleware to support future i18n functionality in Hono applications.
context.author: lord007tn

decision.claim: Ship a new languageDetector middleware imported from hono/language that detects language from query, cookie, header, and path with configurable order.
decision.quote: import { languageDetector } from 'hono/language'
decision.author: lord007tn

decision.claim: Maintainers decided to ship the middleware in the v4.7 minor release.
decision.quote: Sorry for my super late response! Let's prepare to ship this middleware in the next minor version, `v4.7`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The module path hono/language was kept (rather than hono/language-detector) because the exported function name languageDetector makes the feature clear.
decision.quote: I know `language` is too general, but if the function imported from `hono/language` is `languageDetector`, the user can imagine the feature, so I think `hono/language` is ok.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The parser was refactored into a shared utility so both the accepts helper and language middleware reference a common parser.

decisions.title: Accept Uint8Array (not node:buffer Buffer) in c.body() to support binary responses while staying Web-Standards compliant
decisions.summary: The PR author needed to return Buffers from a Hono app but the API did not accept them. The chosen approach is to widen c.body() to accept Uint8Array rather than node:buffer Buffer, since Buffer extends Uint8Array.

context.claim: The PR author needed to return Buffers from a Hono app but the API did not accept them.
context.quote: I needed to be able to return Buffers from my Hono app, and was not able to do so.
context.author: askorupskyy

context.claim: Linked issue #3729 reports that passing a Node Buffer (e.g. from readFileSync) to c.body() produces a TypeScript error because Buffer<ArrayBufferLike> is not assignable to the Data type.
context.quote: Argument of type 'Buffer<ArrayBufferLike>' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Data | null'.
context.author: rtritto

context.claim: The issue suggests adding Buffer<ArrayBufferLike> to the data parameter of c.body.
context.quote: Maybe `Buffer<ArrayBufferLike>` type should be added to data parameter of `c.body`.
context.author: rtritto

decision.claim: The chosen approach is to widen c.body() to accept Uint8Array rather than node:buffer Buffer, since Buffer extends Uint8Array.
decision.quote: looks like `Buffer` indeed is a Node-only construct (they made it before Uint8Array was invented), so to be compliant with Web Standards I moved to `Uint8Array`, which Buffer extends with more methods.
decision.author: askorupskyy

decision.claim: The final commit replaces Buffer with Uint8Array for Web Standards compliance.
decision.quote: fix(hono/context): buffer -> uint8array for web standards
decision.author: askorupskyy

decision.claim: The maintainer approved the final approach, indicating consensus to merge.
decision.quote: Looks good to me! We can merge this.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The core of hono should not depend on node:* modules, since Buffer is not part of the Web Standards API.
forces.quote: It is importing just types, but I think we should not use `node:*` modules in the core of `hono`. Actually, `Buffer` is not in Web Standards API.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Deno does not expose Buffer on the global scope, so typing had to account for runtime differences.
forces.quote: I added this for Deno. Deno does not have a Buffer exposed to the global scope
forces.author: askorupskyy

forces.claim: Uint8Array fits cleanly into the existing BodyInit union type without extending it.
forces.quote:
If I added `Uint8Array`, `_BodyInit` was marked as extended.
So I think looks good to me.
forces.author: EdamAme-x

consequences.claim: Node Buffer values will continue to work because Buffer is an instance of Uint8Array.
consequences.quote: `Buffer.from('') instanceof Uint8Array // true`
consequences.author: askorupskyy

consequences.claim: A test was added to verify Uint8Array support.
consequences.quote: Added one more test and it looks like everything works
consequences.author: askorupskyy

consequences.claim: The maintainer deferred the decision on whether this ships as a patch or minor release.
consequences.quote: I'm considering whether this change should be included in a patch release or a minor release. I'll decide later.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Import Buffer type from node:buffer and add it to the c.body() parameter type
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would introduce a node:* module dependency into hono's core, and Buffer is not part of the Web Standards API.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: It is importing just types, but I think we should not use `node:*` modules in the core of `hono`. Actually, `Buffer` is not in Web Standards API.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Add a helper function to convert node:buffer Buffer into a web-standard body format such as ReadableStream
alternatives.rejection_reason: Proposed by EdamAme-x as an option; not taken because the author instead chose to switch the accepted type to Uint8Array, which subsumes Buffer without requiring a conversion helper.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
It might be a good option to have a helper function to convert from `node:buffer` to the web standard accepted body format (For example, `ReadbleStream`).
If you think it is a good idea, we can change the implementation of this PR.
alternatives.author: EdamAme-x

alternatives.name: Also add URLSearchParams and FormData to the accepted body types
alternatives.rejection_reason: Out of scope for this PR; BodyInit is used by ClientRequestImpl in hc for parsing client input and is not relevant to c.body() return types.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: `BodyInit` is what's used by `ClientRequestImpl`, which is part `hc`. That's to parse whatever input you give to `hc` – this is why your second example doesn't work. I don't think BodyInit matters in the context of this PR
alternatives.author: askorupskyy

decisions.title: Add JWK Auth Middleware (hono/jwk) with support for static keys, async key functions, and jwks_uri fetching
decisions.summary: The author needed a JWK middleware for their projects and believed contributing it to Hono would save time for others. The JWK Auth Middleware was merged into the next branch for the next minor version (v4.7).

context.claim: The author needed a JWK middleware for their projects and believed contributing it to Hono would save time for others.
context.quote: Hello, I recently needed a JWK middleware for my projects, but I figured contributing it to hono has the potential to save me and others a lot of time.
context.author: Beyondo

context.claim: The PR addresses three pre-existing issues requesting JWK support in Hono.
context.quote:
### Addressed issues:
- https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/3658
- https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/2589
- https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/672

decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The middleware supports three ways to provide keys: a static array, an async function, or a jwks_uri URL with optional RequestInit.
decision.quote:
### Middleware Features:
- Set `options.keys` to a static array of public keys `HonoJsonWebKey[]` in code.
- Set `options.keys` to an async function that returns a `Promise<HonoJsonWebKey[]>` for flexibility
- Set `options.jwks_uri` to fetch JWKs from a URI, after which it appends those fetched keys to provided `keys` if any
- Set an optional `init` parameter (only used for `jwks_uri`)—useful if your host supports caching through custom init options.
decision.author: Beyondo

decision.claim: The extended JWK interface was named HonoJsonWebKey rather than ExtendedJsonWebKey.
decision.quote: I also wonder if it should be `HonoJsonWebKey` or `ExtendedJsonWebKey`, but `HonoJsonWebKey` is okay because it's a Hono specific type. Let's go with this.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Code-Hex approved the PR.
decision.quote:
LGTM

@yusukebe
decision.author: Code-Hex

decision.claim: yusukebe approved the PR.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The middleware requires presence of a `kid` field in the JWT header in order to select the correct key, following RFC 7515.
forces.quote: Added `JwtHeaderRequiresKid` exception. Since the middleware requires presence of a `kid` field in the header in order to select the correct key.
forces.author: Beyondo

forces.claim: The JsonWebKey TypeScript type was extended to include `kid?: string` because it is a standard per RFC 7515 Section 4.1.4.
forces.quote: Typescript-extended `JsonWebKey` to have `kid?: string` (This is a standard: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7515#section-4.1.4)
forces.author: Beyondo

forces.claim: Test coverage was initially decreased, and maintainers requested more tests before merging.
