forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Code-Hex suggested adding validation for fetched JWKS keys.
forces.quote: I think you should really add validation, but what do you think?
forces.author: Code-Hex

consequences.claim: A pre-existing bug in Jwt.sign where privateKey.alg was ignored in favor of the alg parameter was fixed as part of this PR.
consequences.quote: Fixed a bug in `Jwt.sign(payload, privateKey, alg)` where `privateKey.alg` was always ignored and only `alg` parameter was considered.
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: Only minimal JWKS key validation (array check) was added; per-key validation was deemed pointless as Jwt.verify handles malformed keys by returning 401.
consequences.quote: Just added a small validation to check if `keys` is an **array**—though iterating through **each key** in that array to validate could be pointless (as-in stateless backends will have to re-do it every request).
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: Signed cookie unit tests for JWK were deferred as not a priority for this PR.
consequences.quote: But they did remind me—we need some **_signed cookie_ unit tests** if we're gonna make sure to support everything in `jwt/index.test.ts` too. Though I don't think it's exactly a priority and could be implemented later.
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: Caching of fetched JWKS is delegated to the host platform via the optional init parameter (e.g., Cloudflare Workers cf options) rather than being built into the middleware.
consequences.quote: Set an optional `init` parameter (only used for `jwks_uri`)—useful if your host supports caching through custom init options.
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: The HonoJsonWebKey type was exported externally from src/utils/jwt/types.ts to make it reusable like JWTPayload.
consequences.quote: Yup! Less confusing this way. Also this reminded me that we should probably export it externally so I did that in `src/utils/jwt/types.ts` since it's already used for `JWTPayload` and other types as well.
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: A reusable `Jwt.verifyFromJwks` utility function was added alongside the middleware for backends that need JWK verification beyond a middleware.
consequences.quote: Added `Jwt.verifyFromJwks` util function _(batteries included)_.
consequences.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Name the extended JWK type `ExtendedJsonWebKey`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected in favor of `HonoJsonWebKey` because the type is Hono-specific; `HonoJsonWebKey` was deemed clearer and less confusing.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I also wonder if it should be `HonoJsonWebKey` or `ExtendedJsonWebKey`, but `HonoJsonWebKey` is okay because it's a Hono specific type. Let's go with this.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Use an actual HTTP server (via @hono/node-server) for JWKS endpoint tests
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected because using a real server on a fixed port causes collisions with other running servers and makes tests fragile; replaced with msw (Mock Service Worker) to mock the HTTP server, matching the approach in src/client/client.test.ts.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: It's better not to use an actual server. For example, if you run another server on the `3000` port, the test will fail since the port is a collision. Using `msw` to mock the HTTP server that is used in `src/client/client.test.ts` is good:
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Per-key validation of each fetched JWKS entry (checking fields like `e`, `alg`, types)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected as pointless because stateless backends would have to re-validate every request, and missing/wrong fields already cause Jwt.verify to return 401; only a lightweight array-type check was added.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Just added a small validation to check if `keys` is an **array**—though iterating through **each key** in that array to validate could be pointless (as-in stateless backends will have to re-do it every request).

For example, missing fields like `e` or `alg` would not cause a problem as that means it would then be a _**different key**_, always resulting in **401** in `Jwt.verify`, while extra fields/metadata are always **ignored** in `Jwt.verify` too.
alternatives.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Remove the default `'HS256'` from Jwt.sign and make `alg` optional, throwing when neither privateKey.alg nor alg is defined
alternatives.rejection_reason: Raised as a suggestion by the author but not adopted in this PR; the default was kept. Author characterized current behavior as potentially misleading but deferred the change.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: **Suggestion:** Removal of the default `'HS256'` value from `Jwt.sign` and make `alg` optional, so that if neither `privateKey.alg` nor `alg` is defined, throw an error. My 'philosophy' for this is that it would be misleading for hono to display `= 'HS256'` when the privateKey can internally have a different algorithm
alternatives.author: Beyondo

decisions.title: Add `generateDigest` option to etag middleware for custom hashing
decisions.summary: The etag middleware was hard-coded to SHA1 and users wanted to supply alternative hashing methods such as wyhash or xxhash. Add a `generateDigest` option to the etag middleware that accepts a `(body: Uint8Array) => ArrayBuffer | Promise<ArrayBuffer>` function.

context.claim: The etag middleware was hard-coded to SHA1 and users wanted to supply alternative hashing methods such as wyhash or xxhash.
context.quote: Allow the user to pass a func to use a custom hashing method that would override `generateDigest`. This currently uses SHA1 maybe the user wants to use wyhash, xxhash, or something else.
context.author: Gobd

context.claim: The current etag implementation is forced to repeat hash generation because crypto.subtle does not support incremental generation.
context.quote: the current etag of hono is limited by the fact that `crypto.subtle` does not support incremental generation, so it is in a situation where it is forced to repeat the generation of the hash value.
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Add a `generateDigest` option to the etag middleware that accepts a `(body: Uint8Array) => ArrayBuffer | Promise<ArrayBuffer>` function.
decision.author: EdamAme-x

decision.claim: The maintainers decided to ship only `generateDigest` in this PR and explicitly deferred a stream-based variant.
decision.quote: I feel that `generateDigestStream` is outside the scope of this PR. So, how about we implement only `generateDigest` in this PR?
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: usualoma agreed to proceed with the generateDigest-only approach.
decision.quote: OK, then, let's keep going!
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: yusukebe approved the final PR.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The API should remain simple — users should generate the etag value simply by passing a custom hash function, without needing to be aware of streams.
forces.quote: This method of PR is very good because users can generate the etag value simply by passing a custom hash function. Users don't need to be aware of the stream, and it's very simple.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Using a hash algorithm other than SHA1 may speed up hash generation, motivating a pluggable digest function.
forces.quote: it might be possible to speed up the hash value generation using an algorithm other than SHA1. Then I think it is good to provide the `generateDigest.`
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The maintainers want to keep the ETag middleware simple.
forces.quote: I want to keep the ETag Middleware simple; only `generateDigest` is okay.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Because the API receives the full body rather than a stream, overhead will be large when the body is returned in multiple chunks.
consequences.quote: It's really simple. However, if the body is returned in multiple chunks (if it's large), the overhead will be large, just like with the current "etag" implementation.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Users who require high-performance ETag logic are expected to implement it themselves rather than relying on this middleware.
consequences.quote: Users who wish to require high-performance ETag logic can implement it by not calculating the response content, so the `generateDigestStream` may not be used often.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The internal parameter was renamed from `generateDigest` to `generator` to avoid shadowing the exported function name.
consequences.quote:
okay, thanks for your advice.
I just changed this to `generator`.
consequences.author: EdamAme-x

alternatives.name: Support both `generateDigest` and `generateDigestStream` options
alternatives.rejection_reason: yusukebe considered the stream variant out of scope and chose to implement only generateDigest to keep the middleware simple.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I feel that `generateDigestStream` is outside the scope of this PR. So, how about we implement only `generateDigest` in this PR?
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Introduce an API modeled on Cloudflare's `crypto.DigestStream`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would organize the API more neatly, but there was no motivation to pursue it.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think there are also ways to introduce APIs like Cloudflare's `crypto.DigestStream`. However, I don't think we have the motivation to do that.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Do not add the feature; document a middleware-wrapper pattern for users instead
alternatives.rejection_reason: usualoma proposed skipping the feature and guiding users to wrap etag in their own middleware, but yusukebe ultimately decided generateDigest was worth providing because alternative algorithms can speed up hash generation.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I considered it, and the `generateDigest` feature is good. As @usualoma says, using a simple value is faster than generating the hash value. However, as @Gobd says, it might be possible to speed up the hash value generation using an algorithm other than SHA1. Then I think it is good to provide the `generateDigest.`
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Stream-based generator API — `(stream: ReadableStream<Uint8Array>) => string | null | Promise<string | null>`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered out of scope for this PR and rejected in favor of keeping the ETag middleware simple; users needing high-performance ETag logic can implement it themselves.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I feel that `generateDigestStream` is outside the scope of this PR. So, how about we implement only `generateDigest` in this PR?
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Expose a custom-middleware generator that consumes `c.res.clone().body` as a ReadableStream (usualoma's prototype)
alternatives.rejection_reason: If the API is that flexible, usualoma argued it is better handled as per-user middleware rather than as a built-in option; the team chose the simpler generateDigest option instead.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: However, if you are going to be this flexible, it seems better to handle it in the middleware for each user rather than as an option.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Skip compression for server-sent events in compress middleware
decisions.summary: Using compress middleware with streamSSE causes SSE messages to be buffered instead of streamed Skip compression entirely for responses with content type text/event-stream

context.claim: Using compress middleware with streamSSE causes SSE messages to be buffered instead of streamed
context.quote: Using both `compress` middleware and the `streamSSE` helper from `hono/streaming` doesn't work correctly -- the SSE messages are not streamed, instead they get buffered by the compression mechanism.
context.author: dmitri-gb

context.claim: Compressing SSE would require manual flushing support in the underlying compression mechanism
context.quote: According to [expressjs/compression](https://github.com/expressjs/compression#server-sent-events) it should be possible to compress server-sent events, if the underlying compression mechanism supports manual _flushing_.
context.author: dmitri-gb

context.claim: CompressionStream does not offer manual flushing
context.quote: As far as I can tell though, `CompressionStream` (used by Hono's `compress` middleware) doesn't offer this functionality
context.author: dmitri-gb

decision.claim: Skip compression entirely for responses with content type text/event-stream
decision.quote: so the best we can do is not compress SSE streams at all.
decision.author: dmitri-gb

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the fix approach
decision.quote: I also realized that the problem and your idea are good!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: PR approved and merged
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: SSE requires incremental delivery of messages, not buffered output
forces.quote: the SSE messages are not streamed, instead they get buffered by the compression mechanism.
forces.author: dmitri-gb

forces.claim: Web standard CompressionStream API lacks manual flush capability
forces.quote: `CompressionStream` (used by Hono's `compress` middleware) doesn't offer this functionality
forces.author: dmitri-gb

forces.claim: Test should live in compress middleware's test file since the fix belongs to compress middleware
forces.quote: I think it's a little unsuitable if you add it to `src/helper/streaming/sse.test.tsx` because this fix is about Compress Middleware. So it will be okay to test if it will not be compressed if the content type is `text/event-stream` in `src/middleware/compress/index.test.ts`.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: SSE responses will not be compressed at all, losing potential bandwidth savings
consequences.quote: so the best we can do is not compress SSE streams at all.
consequences.author: dmitri-gb

consequences.claim: Could be revisited if CompressionStream gains flush control in the future
consequences.quote:
related issues for tracking ability to control flushing on `CompressionStream`:
- https://github.com/whatwg/compression/issues/56
- https://github.com/whatwg/compression/issues/22
consequences.author: mfulton26

alternatives.name: Compress SSE streams with manual flushing (as expressjs/compression does)
alternatives.rejection_reason: CompressionStream, the underlying web-standard API used by Hono's compress middleware, does not expose manual flush functionality, so this approach isn't feasible.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: According to [expressjs/compression](https://github.com/expressjs/compression#server-sent-events) it should be possible to compress server-sent events, if the underlying compression mechanism supports manual _flushing_. As far as I can tell though, `CompressionStream` (used by Hono's `compress` middleware) doesn't offer this functionality, so the best we can do is not compress SSE streams at all.
alternatives.author: dmitri-gb

decisions.title: Skip attaching AbortSignal in streaming helpers on Bun >= 1.1.27 (uses ReadableStream.cancel)
decisions.summary: A memory leak investigation revealed that Hono's streaming helpers unconditionally attach an AbortSignal to each request. Hono will skip attaching the AbortSignal on newer Bun versions, gating the behavior behind an `isOldBunVersion` check.

context.claim: A memory leak investigation revealed that Hono's streaming helpers unconditionally attach an AbortSignal to each request.
context.quote: While investigating a memory leak, I noticed that the streams helpers in hono unconditionally attach an AbortSignal to each request.
context.author: Jarred-Sumner

context.claim: As of Bun v1.1.27, Bun calls `cancel` on ReadableStream, making the AbortSignal attachment unnecessary on newer Bun versions.
context.quote: As of Bun v1.1.27, [we call `cancel` on ReadableStream](https://bun.sh/blog/bun-v1.1.27#the-cancel-method-in-readablestream) so this should be unnecessary.
context.author: Jarred-Sumner

decision.claim: Hono will skip attaching the AbortSignal on newer Bun versions, gating the behavior behind an `isOldBunVersion` check.
decision.quote: refactor(helper/streaming): Avoid attaching AbortSignal on newer versions of Bun
decision.author: Jarred-Sumner

decision.claim: Maintainer unified the version-check logic into a single function in utils.ts and merged the change.
decision.quote: I fixed the tests and unified the functions as one function in `utils.ts.` Looks good. I'll merge this.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Behavior must remain correct on old Bun versions (before 1.1.27) where ReadableStream.cancel is not called and the AbortSignal is still needed.
forces.quote: I verified that on Bun v1.1.26, hardcoding the `isOldBunVersion` function to return false caused the abort-related tests to hang
forces.author: Jarred-Sumner

forces.claim: The new behavior must be verified against current Bun versions where ReadableStream.cancel is available.
forces.quote: on Bun v1.2 hardcoding the `isOldBunVersion` function to return false those tests pass.
forces.author: Jarred-Sumner

consequences.claim: The change introduces duplicate code, which the author accepted as preferable to a more invasive refactor.
consequences.quote: There is duplicate code but it felt less invasive to duplicate the code.
consequences.author: Jarred-Sumner

consequences.claim: Tests for the abort-signal behavior now require emulating an old Bun version via a global override to continue exercising the legacy code path.
consequences.quote: To pass the test, specify the old version at the global.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: During merge, the duplicated version-check logic was consolidated into a single shared utility.
consequences.quote: I fixed the tests and unified the functions as one function in `utils.ts.`
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Deduplicate the streaming helper code instead of duplicating the version-gated branches
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author considered unifying the code but judged duplication to be less invasive than the refactor required to share it; the maintainer later did a partial unification of just the version-check helper at merge time.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: There is duplicate code but it felt less invasive to duplicate the code.
alternatives.author: Jarred-Sumner

decisions.title: Add `cacheableStatusCodes` option to cache middleware and restrict default cacheable status to 200 only
decisions.summary: The existing cache middleware caches any response where response.ok is true, i.e., all 2xx status codes including 201, 202, 205, and 206. The default set of cacheable status codes is narrowed to just 200, and a new `cacheableStatusCodes` option lets users opt into additional codes.

context.claim: The existing cache middleware caches any response where response.ok is true, i.e., all 2xx status codes including 201, 202, 205, and 206.
context.quote: Regarding the status code, currently, `response.ok` is used, i.e., 200-299 is cached, but I think it should be narrowed down more.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: Caching certain 2xx codes such as 206 requires additional handling (e.g., including the Range header in the cache key) that the middleware does not support by default, creating a risk of cache accidents or vulnerabilities.
context.quote: For example, 206 is cacheable, as you know from the HTTP semantics, but to cache it, we must include the Range request header in the cache key, etc., and Hono's cache middleware does not support this by default.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: The original PR was motivated by wanting to avoid caching responses defined as uncacheable by RFC 7231.
context.quote: This pull request updates the cache middleware to avoid caching when it is defined as uncacheable in RFC 7231.
context.author: miyamo2

decision.claim: Cacheability based on request method was removed from the middleware; method filtering should be done via Hono's routing helpers (e.g., app.get()).
decision.quote: Also, the cacheability of the request method has been removed.
decision.author: miyamo2

decision.claim: yusukebe endorsed 200 as the default with `cacheableStatusCodes` as the opt-in extension mechanism.
decision.quote: The. 200 as default is suitable. If the user wants to add additional code, using the ` cacheableStatusCodes` option is good.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The `cacheableStatusCodes` option is typed as StatusCode[] rather than number[].
decision.quote: modified `cacheableStatusCodes` to []StatusCode instead of []number
decision.author: miyamo2

decision.claim: The default set of cacheable status codes is narrowed to just 200, and a new `cacheableStatusCodes` option lets users opt into additional codes.
decision.quote: I modified the default cached response to only `200: OK`.
decision.author: miyamo2

forces.claim: RFC 7231 distinguishes 'cacheable' (permissible) from 'should be cached' (policy), so the framework must make a policy choice rather than blindly follow the RFC list.
forces.quote: the RFC talks about ‘whether or not it is cacheable?’ and not ‘whether or not it should be cached?’ , so I think it's up to the ‘application (or framework)’ to decide whether or not middleware should cache 404s.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Backward compatibility is a strong constraint: changing the default cached status range is a breaking change and cannot be merged immediately.
forces.quote: Either way, making the default status code not 200 - 299 will cause a breaking change. We can't merge this immediately.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Hono already provides method filtering via its router (e.g., app.get()), making method-level configuration in the cache middleware redundant.
forces.quote: As mentioned above, hono can specify the method to apply middleware in the framework semantics. (`app.get(‘*’, cache())`) With that in mind, I think specifying it in the cache middleware is unnecessary.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Defaults should favor preventing accidents and vulnerabilities from careless caching.
forces.quote: From the perspective of preventing accidents (or certain types of vulnerability) caused by careless caching, the default should be 200 only.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Implementation should use Set<number>/set.has() rather than Map<number, boolean>/map.get() for both clarity and performance.
forces.quote: we should use the combination of `Set<number>` and `set.has()` here, rather than the combination of `Map<number, boolean>` and `map.get()`. It is more straightforward for what we want to do, and also improves performance.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: The default cached status set becomes 200 only; users who previously relied on 2xx caching must opt in via cacheableStatusCodes.
consequences.quote: I modified the default cached response to only `200: OK`.
consequences.author: miyamo2

consequences.claim: Because this is a breaking default change, the PR was scheduled for a future minor release rather than merged immediately.
consequences.quote: This is a new feature, so this may be included in the next minor version. So, I left it does not merge immediately.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users wanting method-scoped caching must rely on Hono's routing (e.g., app.get('*', cache())) rather than a middleware option.
consequences.quote: Regarding handling the method, I also think the middleware should not specify the method, but for example, you can use `app.get()` to support only `GET.` We don't have to implement that in the middleware.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The PR's original title/scope (avoiding uncacheable responses per RFC) was renamed to reflect the narrower final feature.
consequences.quote: I fixed the title to "add `cacheableStatusCodes` option" since the purpose was changed from when you created the PR.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Restrict cacheable request methods inside the cache middleware (only GET/HEAD, or GET/HEAD/POST/PATCH per RFC)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Hono's routing already lets users restrict the method (app.get('*', cache())), so method filtering in the middleware is redundant; also, some apps legitimately want to cache non-standard methods like DELETE, which an in-middleware filter would block.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: As mentioned above, hono can specify the method to apply middleware in the framework semantics. (`app.get(‘*’, cache())`) With that in mind, I think specifying it in the cache middleware is unnecessary.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Leave the current simple implementation unchanged (possibly just excluding 201/202/205)
alternatives.rejection_reason: usualoma raised this as a viable option since there's no security issue with the current behavior, but the authors chose instead to narrow the default to 200 and add the opt-in option.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Therefore, although it is a very thought-provoking pull request, I think it is also possible to leave the current simple implementation as it is. (It might be a good idea to exclude 201, 202, 205, etc.)
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Implement the cacheable-status lookup with Map<number, boolean> and map.get()
alternatives.rejection_reason: Set<number>/set.has() is more straightforward for membership checks and performs better.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: we should use the combination of `Set<number>` and `set.has()` here, rather than the combination of `Map<number, boolean>` and `map.get()`. It is more straightforward for what we want to do, and also improves performance.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Use the RFC 7231 default cacheable set (200, 203, 204, 206, 300, 301, 404, 405, 410, 414, 501) as the default
alternatives.rejection_reason: RFC 7231 defines what is 'cacheable' (permissible), not what 'should be cached' (policy); caching 404 by default, for example, is application-specific and often wants a different TTL, so the framework default should be more conservative.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think it's a good idea to refer to the RFC, but the RFC talks about ‘whether or not it is cacheable?’ and not ‘whether or not it should be cached?’ , so I think it's up to the ‘application (or framework)’ to decide whether or not middleware should cache 404s.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Keep caching the full 2xx range (Response.ok behavior)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Codes like 206 require extra cache-key handling (e.g., Range header) that the middleware doesn't support, so caching all 2xx risks accidents and vulnerabilities; the default should be narrowed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Regarding the status code, currently, `response.ok` is used, i.e., 200-299 is cached, but I think it should be narrowed down more.

For example, 206 is cacheable, as you know from the HTTP semantics, but to cache it, we must include the Range request header in the cache key, etc., and Hono's cache middleware does not support this by default.

From the perspective of preventing accidents (or certain types of vulnerability) caused by careless caching, the default should be 200 only.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Type `cacheableStatusCodes` as number[]
alternatives.rejection_reason: The existing StatusCode type in src/utils/http-status.ts is a better fit and gives stronger typing than plain number.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: You might use `StatusCode` in `src/utils/http-status.ts` instead of `number`
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Extend hono/jwk middleware with allow_anon option and pass Context to callbacks
decisions.summary: Users requested the allow_anon and Context-passing features in the original jwk PR The jwk middleware now supports an allow_anon option, a function-form jwks_uri, Context in all callbacks, and sync-or-async callbacks

context.claim: Users requested the allow_anon and Context-passing features in the original jwk PR
context.quote: Adding two main useful features people have been asking for in the [original PR](https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3826) + some slight changes.
context.author: Beyondo

context.claim: The allow_anon feature stems from a request in comment 2656576941 on PR #3826
context.quote:
**Allow anonymous requests** by setting the new **`allow_anon`** option to `true`
  - https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3826#issuecomment-2656576941
context.author: Beyondo

context.claim: The Context-in-callbacks feature stems from a request in comment 2687416251 on PR #3826
context.quote:
**`Context`** is now **included in all callbacks**, so you can **e.g** pull information from `c.env` such as auth server
  - https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3826#issuecomment-2687416251
context.author: Beyondo

decision.claim: The jwk middleware now supports an allow_anon option, a function-form jwks_uri, Context in all callbacks, and sync-or-async callbacks
decision.quote:
### New features:
- **Allow anonymous requests** by setting the new **`allow_anon`** option to `true`
  - https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3826#issuecomment-2656576941
- **Optional callback for the `jwks_uri`** instead of only a hard-coded string
- **`Context`** is now **included in all callbacks**, so you can **e.g** pull information from `c.env` such as auth server
  - https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/3826#issuecomment-2687416251
- Callbacks now can be either **async or sync**
decision.author: Beyondo

decision.claim: Callbacks were removed from Jwt.verifyFromJwks as part of the same change
decision.quote: This PR breaks no code at all except for `Jwt.verifyFromJwks(...)`—specifically removed callbacks support from it since they're redundant and can be called outside, and also since only the middleware can provide the `Context` variable to the callbacks.
decision.author: Beyondo

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the PR for merge into the next branch
decision.quote: It looks good! I'll merge this later into the next branch for the next minor version and release it.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Only the middleware can provide Context to callbacks, so callback logic must live there rather than in Jwt.verifyFromJwks
forces.quote: Jwt.verifyFromJwks cannot provide the Context to the new callbacks—only the middleware can
forces.author: Beyondo

forces.claim: Hono requires exp and iat in the JWT payload at verify stage, which makes jwtPayload a reliable authenticated-vs-anonymous indicator
forces.quote: hono requires requests to have `exp` and `iat` in the payload (in the `verify` stage) which means the payload would always be defined for authenticated requests and be reliably used as indicator.
forces.author: Beyondo

forces.claim: Callbacks in Jwt.verifyFromJwks are redundant because values like jwks_uri/keys can be resolved by the caller beforehand
forces.quote: It’s always called from functions, so `jwks_uri`/`keys` can be grabbed before calling `Jwt.verifyFromJwks` anyways
forces.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: The change is breaking for Jwt.verifyFromJwks consumers that relied on its callback form
consequences.quote: This PR breaks no code at all except for `Jwt.verifyFromJwks(...)`—specifically removed callbacks support from it
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: Setting allow_anon=true allows requests without a token in header or cookie to pass through the middleware successfully
consequences.quote: Setting `allow_anon` to `true` means requests without a token present (in either header/cookie) would be allowed to pass through the middleware successfully.
consequences.author: Beyondo

consequences.claim: A single endpoint can now serve both authenticated and anonymous requests, distinguished via c.get('jwtPayload')
consequences.quote: This PR allows having a single endpoint for both authenticated and non-authenticated requests through `allow_anon`. The variable `c.get("jwtPayload")` can be used **to differentiate authenticated requests from anonymous requests**
consequences.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Require callbacks to be async only
alternatives.rejection_reason: Relaxed so callbacks may be either async or sync, avoiding unnecessary Promise wrapping for synchronous logic.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Callbacks now can be either **async or sync**
alternatives.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Name the option skip_if_no_token
alternatives.rejection_reason: Renamed to allow_anon for readability.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: renamed new option for readability: skip_if_no_token -> allow_anon
alternatives.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Keep callback support on Jwt.verifyFromJwks
alternatives.rejection_reason: Callbacks on Jwt.verifyFromJwks are redundant since callers can resolve jwks_uri/keys beforehand, and Jwt.verifyFromJwks cannot provide the Context that the new callbacks depend on.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Removed function callbacks from `Jwt.verifyFromJwks` for two reasons:
1. It’s always called from functions, so `jwks_uri`/`keys` can be grabbed before calling `Jwt.verifyFromJwks` anyways
2. Jwt.verifyFromJwks cannot provide the Context to the new callbacks—only the middleware can
alternatives.author: Beyondo

alternatives.name: Keep jwks_uri as a hard-coded string only
alternatives.rejection_reason: Supporting a callback form lets consumers derive the URI from runtime context (e.g., c.env.authServer) rather than being locked to a compile-time string.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: **Optional callback for the `jwks_uri`** instead of only a hard-coded string
alternatives.author: Beyondo

decisions.title: Introduce async getColorEnabledAsync() using dynamic import of 'cloudflare:workers' to support NO_COLOR on Cloudflare Workers
decisions.summary: Cloudflare Workers did not respect NO_COLOR in the logger because the existing getColorEnabled() could not see Worker environment variables. Logger uses a new asynchronous getColorEnabledAsync() that retrieves Cloudflare env via dynamic import of 'cloudflare:workers'.

context.claim: process.env is already available on Cloudflare Workers with the nodejs_compat flag, raising a question about whether native support was strictly needed.
context.quote:
Currently, `process.env` is available on Cloudflare Workers with `nodejs_compat` flag, so there may not be a need to support it. 
> https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/nodejs/process/#processenv
context.author: ryuapp

context.claim: Cloudflare Workers did not respect NO_COLOR in the logger because the existing getColorEnabled() could not see Worker environment variables.
context.quote:
Can something be added to check it this is running in a cloudflare worker to disable coloring?

My logs spit out [32m200[0m 923ms, which is terrible.

The `c.env` could be passed in and if `NO_COLOR === true` then we are set.
context.author: kingmesal

context.claim: Cloudflare Workers can now expose env as a global import, which makes it possible to read NO_COLOR without nodejs_compat flags.
context.quote:
Also, since `env` on Cloudflare can now be imported in global, it may be possible to implement it without compat flags. I'll check it later.
https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/bindings/#importing-env-as-a-global
context.author: ryuapp

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the implementation.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The original synchronous getColorEnabled() is retained for backwards compatibility with showRoute().
decision.quote: `getColorEnabled()` is maintained for backwards compatibility for `showRoute()`.
decision.author: ryuapp

decision.claim: Logger uses a new asynchronous getColorEnabledAsync() that retrieves Cloudflare env via dynamic import of 'cloudflare:workers'.
decision.quote: Logger now uses `getColorEnabledAsync()` to support retrieving cloudflare env. Since we couldn't support this without dynamic import now, I created a new `getColorEnabled()` that is asynchronous.
decision.author: ryuapp

forces.claim: Reviewer asked to consolidate with existing getColorEnabled() logic to avoid duplication.
forces.quote: The `getColorEnabled` has some similar logic, so how about the following implementation?
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Maintainer flagged the change as tricky (dynamic import + calling env), requiring runtime tests.
forces.quote: I think this code is doing a little bit tricky things: dynamic import and calling `env`. So it's better to write runtime tests.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The implementation needed to remain backward-compatible with older Cloudflare Workers that may lack 'cloudflare:workers' import.
forces.quote: 'NO_COLOR' in ((await import('cloudflare:workers')).env ?? {}) // ?? {} is for backward compat
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Reading Cloudflare Workers env without compat flags requires dynamic import of the 'cloudflare:workers' module, which is inherently asynchronous.
forces.quote: Since we couldn't support this without dynamic import now, I created a new `getColorEnabled()` that is asynchronous.
forces.author: ryuapp

consequences.claim: Two public utility functions now exist: sync getColorEnabled() and async getColorEnabledAsync(), splitting the API by call-site constraints.
consequences.quote: `getColorEnabled()` is maintained for backwards compatibility for `showRoute()`.
consequences.author: ryuapp

consequences.claim: The dynamic import of 'cloudflare:workers' causes a bundling/runtime error reported by a user in a Next.js monorepo setup with Hono v4.8.0.
consequences.quote: got this error from this line:
consequences.author: ysm-dev

consequences.claim: The cost of the dynamic import has not been measured and may need follow-up optimization.
consequences.quote:
The cost of dynamic imports has not been investigated, so it may be necessary to optimize it with following PR: 
- https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4006
consequences.author: ryuapp

alternatives.name: Duplicate the sync NO_COLOR detection logic inside getColorEnabledAsync()
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer pointed out the logic overlapped with getColorEnabled() and proposed delegating to !getColorEnabled() for non-Cloudflare branches; the author adopted the consolidated version.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: The `getColorEnabled` has some similar logic, so how about the following implementation?
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Pass c.env into the logger to read NO_COLOR from Worker bindings
alternatives.rejection_reason: Original issue proposal to thread c.env through the logger; implementation instead used a global dynamic import of 'cloudflare:workers' so no API change to the logger signature was needed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: The `c.env` could be passed in and if `NO_COLOR === true` then we are set.
alternatives.author: kingmesal

alternatives.name: Rely on process.env via Cloudflare's nodejs_compat flag (do nothing in Hono)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Was noted as a viable user-side workaround but the author proceeded with first-class support; requiring users to enable nodejs_compat was considered an obscure approach in the linked issue.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Currently, `process.env` is available on Cloudflare Workers with `nodejs_compat` flag, so there may not be a need to support it.
alternatives.author: ryuapp

alternatives.name: Alternative implementation proposed in PR #4095
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author mentioned an alternative plan in PR #4095 but proceeded with the dynamic-import approach in this PR; the sibling PR was not taken.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I also have another plan, #4095.
alternatives.author: ryuapp

decisions.title: Support http+unix scheme in getPath URL parsing
decisions.summary: Deno.serve passes URLs with the http+unix scheme to the Request object when a Unix socket path is specified Adopt a conditional offset check that branches on the 10th character to detect http+unix and start the path search at index 13 instead of 8

context.claim: Bun and node-server do not use the http+unix scheme
context.quote: `bun` and `node-server` do not use it.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: yusukebe was previously unaware URLs in Request could have the http+unix schema
context.quote: I didn't know that the URL in the Request may have an `http+unix` schema.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: http+unix is rarely used on the server side but commonly used on the client side
context.quote: `http+unix` is often used on the client side (e.g., curl), but it is very rarely used on the server side.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: Deno.serve passes URLs with the http+unix scheme to the Request object when a Unix socket path is specified
context.quote: in Deno, when you specify `path` in `Deno.serve`, a string like `http+unix://%2Ftmp%2Fsocket%2Esock/your/path` is passed to the URL of the Request object.
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Adopt a conditional offset check that branches on the 10th character to detect http+unix and start the path search at index 13 instead of 8
decision.quote: `url.charCodeAt(9) === 58 ? 13 : 8` seems to offer the best performance.
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: yusukebe approved merging the fixed offset approach because http+unix adoption is likely to grow via Deno.serve
decision.quote: I approved this PR. I think `http+unix` will be used more because `Deno.serve` uses it. So fixing `8` is not a bad idea.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The getPath hot path must maintain minimal overhead since it runs on every request
forces.quote: Compared to the current situation, the performance is reduced by only a few tens of ps. However, since this is not a frequently used feature, it seems wasteful to incur several tens of ps of overhead for this purpose.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The default behavior should Just Work for users unfamiliar with the getPath option
forces.quote: However, the user who is not familiar with it (almost all users) will be surprised by that.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Deno.serve with Unix domain sockets should work without requiring users to configure getPath
forces.quote: I think it would benefit Hono if `Deno.serve` using Unix domain sockets would work without specifying the `getPath` option.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Performance degrades by only a few tens of picoseconds compared to the prior fixed-offset implementation
consequences.quote: Compared to the current situation, the performance is reduced by only a few tens of ps.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Future compatibility is not guaranteed — a new scheme like http+socket would require an individual code change
consequences.quote: if a scheme such as `http+socket` is introduced in the future, individual responses will be necessary, so future compatibility is not guaranteed with this code.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: The change is no worse than the status quo, which also did not handle non-http(s) schemes
consequences.quote: since the current code fixed at `8` does not support this either, I don't think it will be any worse than the current situation.
consequences.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Require users to pass a custom getPath option (e.g. `(req) => new URL(req.url).pathname`)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Most users are unfamiliar with the getPath option and would be surprised when Deno.serve over Unix sockets didn't work out-of-the-box.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: the user can handle it with the useful `getPath` option, just write this... However, the user who is not familiar with it (almost all users) will be surprised by that.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: getPathA: fixed offset `url.indexOf('/', 8)` (status quo)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Does not handle the http+unix scheme — the path search starts mid-scheme and returns the wrong index.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: the current code fixed at `8` does not support this either
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: getPathC: generic `url.indexOf('/', url.indexOf(':') + 3)` scheme-length lookup
alternatives.rejection_reason: Benchmarked as slower than the charCodeAt-based branch; the conditional offset approach (getPathB) offered the best performance.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I compared using the following benchmark script, and `url.charCodeAt(9) === 58 ? 13 : 8` seems to offer the best performance.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Fall back to URL.pathname parsing whenever the scheme is not http or https
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered as the ideal generic solution, but no good implementation idea was available — likely due to the performance cost of constructing a URL object per request.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ideally, it falls back to using `URL.pathname` if the schema is not `http` or `https` in `getPath`. But I don't have a good implementation idea.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Add plugin system to SSG helper with beforeRequest/afterResponse/afterGenerate hooks

context.claim: Octocov keys comment updates on the configured 'repository' value, so without separate configs the tsc and tsgo benchmark comments would collide.

decisions.title: Add fire() to service-worker adapter with fetch:undefined default for base-fire compatibility
decisions.summary: The fire() addition follows from a prior discussion about service-worker adapter use cases including non-service-worker environments like webassembly componentizejs. Introduce fire() in the service-worker adapter that calls addEventListener('fetch', handle(app)).

context.claim: The fire() addition follows from a prior discussion about service-worker adapter use cases including non-service-worker environments like webassembly componentizejs.
context.quote: The background: https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/3127#issuecomment-2843529188
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The default fallback of handle to trigger a fetch causes the wasm module to make another fetch in webassembly componentizejs environments, which makes sense in a service worker but not a web server.
context.quote: the default fallback of `handle` to trigger a fetch results in the wasm module making another fetch ([see discussion on issue](https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/3127#issuecomment-2843529188)). That default makes sense in a service worker but not a web server.
context.author: rajsite

decision.claim: Introduce fire() in the service-worker adapter that calls addEventListener('fetch', handle(app)).
decision.quote: This PR introduces the `fire()` method in the Service Worker adapter. It will do `addEventListener('fetch', handle(app))` for the given Hono instance.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: fire() accepts HandleOptions whose fetch defaults to undefined, making it compatible with base fire and suitable for web-server use cases by default.
decision.author: rajsite

decision.claim: The author adopted passing options through to handle() as the implementation approach.
decision.quote: I like this way: https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4214/commits/5be78838669ea6b26caea987dcf6ffa7efadeca6
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Reviewer approved after testing the branch locally.
decision.quote: Tested the branch locally and works great! Thanks for the discussions and consideration!
decision.author: rajsite

forces.claim: The service-worker adapter is being used in non-service-worker environments such as Cloudflare Workers and WebAssembly via componentizejs.
forces.quote: Some of the use-cases discussed in the issue were leveraging the service worker adapter for additional environments ([cloudflare-workers](https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/3127#issuecomment-2456912838) and webassembly [via componentizejs](https://github.com/bytecodealliance/ComponentizeJS?tab=readme-ov-file#using-starlingmonkeys-fetch-event)).
forces.author: rajsite

forces.claim: fire() should be compatible with the existing base fire behavior, which does not fall back to fetch.
forces.quote: If `fire` here is intended to replace the [base fire](https://github.com/honojs/hono/blob/c18c2b2e0992d15e530a69c7db6b9ccbd6f06e61/src/hono-base.ts#L506-L520) behavior (note does not fallback to fetch) / is for web server use cases I'd instead pass undefined explicitly
forces.author: rajsite

forces.claim: For usage in both service-worker and web-server contexts, handle options should be pass-through-able.
forces.quote: if `fire` should be used for both service worker and web server cases I'd make sure to passthrough the handle options
forces.author: rajsite

consequences.claim: Users who need the service-worker fallback-fetch behavior must explicitly override the default by passing options.
consequences.author: rajsite

consequences.claim: The @ts-expect-error is retained because addEventListener is not typed well in ServiceWorker-like contexts (TypeScript issue #14877).
consequences.quote: // @ts-expect-error addEventListener is not typed well in ServiceWorker-like contexts, see: https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/14877
consequences.author: rajsite

alternatives.name: Hardcode fire() to always call handle(app, {fetch: undefined}) with no options parameter
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would prevent service-worker users who want the fallback fetch behavior from configuring it; the author instead chose the options-with-default variant that passes options through to handle().
alternatives.author: rajsite

alternatives.name: Two separate methods: fire() (base-fire compatible) and fireWithFallback(opts) (service-worker fallback behavior)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author preferred a single fire() with a default options object ({fetch: undefined}) over splitting into two methods.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
another idea could be to have separate methods like:
- `fire()`: which is compatible with `base fire` and passes `handle(app, {fetch: undefined})`
- and `fireWithFallback(opts)`: which can be configure for either case and uses the service worker default fallback fetch behavior
alternatives.author: rajsite

alternatives.name: fire(app) with no options, using handle's default fetch fallback
alternatives.rejection_reason: The default fallback-to-fetch behavior makes sense in a service worker but causes wasm modules to make another fetch in componentizejs/web-server contexts, and is incompatible with base fire which does not fall back to fetch.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: the default fallback of `handle` to trigger a fetch results in the wasm module making another fetch ([see discussion on issue](https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/3127#issuecomment-2843529188)). That default makes sense in a service worker but not a web server.
alternatives.author: rajsite

decisions.title: Assign 'cloudflare:workers' to a variable to evade bundler scheme-resolution checks
decisions.summary: Popular bundlers like esbuild and webpack check packages and apply optimizations, but 'cloudflare:workers' is often not supported by default, causing build failures. Change the 'cloudflare:workers' literal to a variable name in the dynamic import so bundler optimization checks do not see a resolvable module specifier.

context.claim: Popular bundlers like esbuild and webpack check packages and apply optimizations, but 'cloudflare:workers' is often not supported by default, causing build failures.
context.quote: Popular bundlers(`esbuild`, `webpack`) check packages and provide optimizations, but `cloudflare:workers` is often not supported by default.
context.author: ryuapp

context.claim: Webpack builds in Next.js failed with an UnhandledSchemeError when encountering 'cloudflare:workers'.
context.quote: Module build failed: UnhandledSchemeError: Reading from "cloudflare:workers" is not handled by plugins (Unhandled scheme).
context.author: ysm-dev

context.claim: The issue also breaks react-router v7 builds, not only Next.js.
context.quote: It's also breaking react-router v7 build.
context.author: cayter

context.claim: Bun builds also failed to resolve 'cloudflare:workers' when building projects that use Hono.
context.quote:
error: Could not resolve: "cloudflare:workers". Maybe you need to "bun install"?
    at /node_modules/hono/dist/utils/color.js:9:121
context.author: Gobd

decision.claim: Change the 'cloudflare:workers' literal to a variable name in the dynamic import so bundler optimization checks do not see a resolvable module specifier.
decision.quote: In this PR, to avoid these optimization checks, change a package name to a variable name.
decision.author: ryuapp

decision.claim: The maintainer endorsed the variable-assignment approach and chose it over bundler-specific ignore comments before merging.
decision.quote: I think the solution of assigning `'cloudflare:workers'` to a variable is good. And, for the minimal, it's okay to only have the `esbuild` test. I like this approach rather than [the approach in the comment](https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4234#pullrequestreview-2941954213), because the implementation should not have these bundler-specific words like `@vite-ignore`.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The implementation should not contain bundler-specific pragmas such as '@vite-ignore'.
forces.quote: the implementation should not have these bundler-specific words like `@vite-ignore`.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: esbuild does not support the same ignore-comment syntax used by webpack/vite, limiting comment-based workarounds.
forces.quote: It's better to ignore errors using comments, but esbuild does not have the same comment.
forces.author: ryuapp

forces.claim: An automated regression test against esbuild was required to prevent reoccurrence.
forces.quote: This PR adds a test to check whether these issues can be avoided in `esbuild`.
forces.author: ryuapp

consequences.claim: Webpack is not covered by automated tests in this PR; it was only verified manually via an external repository.
consequences.quote: We should also add `webpack` tests, but I didn't have a smart way. `webpack` has been checked to work with at least [this repository](https://github.com/ryuapp/check-color-next).
consequences.author: ryuapp

consequences.claim: The variable-assignment workaround does not resolve the error for Bun.build, which still fails to resolve 'cloudflare:workers'.
consequences.author: dan-ol

consequences.claim: A follow-up PR (#4239) was opened to address the remaining Bun.build failure.
consequences.quote: `Bun.build` error, work on https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4239.
consequences.author: ryuapp

decisions.title: Add issuer (iss) claim validation to JWT verify via a unified VerifyOptions object
decisions.summary: JWT verification previously did not validate the issuer (iss) claim. Rather than adding a positional `issuer` parameter to verify(), the API was changed to accept a single VerifyOptions object combined with overload support for the legacy alg-as-string form.

context.claim: JWT verification previously did not validate the issuer (iss) claim.
context.quote:
This adds validation for the issuer claim (`iss`),
with support for simple strings and complex Regex patterns.
context.author: yolocat-dev

context.claim: The change is designed to be opt-in and non-breaking: verification only runs when the user provides an expected issuer.
context.quote: As this only verifies when the user has added an expected issuer, this isn't a breaking change and won't impact usage. You can however opt-in to issuer verification by simply setting the `issuer` option to your expected issuer.
context.author: yolocat-dev

decision.claim: Rather than adding a positional `issuer` parameter to verify(), the API was changed to accept a single VerifyOptions object combined with overload support for the legacy alg-as-string form.
decision.quote: Do you think it will be necessary to verify `sub`, `aud`, and others in the future? If so, the number of arguments will increase accordingly. Therefore, I think it would be better to combine the options into a single object.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The author agreed to implement the unified VerifyOptions approach.
decision.quote:
This looks great, should I implement this or do you want to do that? I could take a look at some other claims and add them accordingly.

Edit: I'll implement it either way ;)
decision.author: yolocat-dev

decision.claim: The maintainer confirmed the direction and the author landed a commit changing the argument to generic verification options.
decision.quote: feat(jwt): changed argument to generic verification options
decision.author: yolocat-dev

decision.claim: The PR was approved by the maintainer.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The API must remain backwards compatible — existing callers passing alg as a string should continue to work.
forces.quote: And support both the new and old option patterns by using overload.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Future claim validations (sub, aud, etc.) are anticipated, so argument growth needs to be managed now.
forces.quote: Do you think it will be necessary to verify `sub`, `aud`, and others in the future? If so, the number of arguments will increase accordingly.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Issuer matching must support both exact strings and regex patterns.
forces.quote: with support for simple strings and complex Regex patterns.
forces.author: yolocat-dev

forces.claim: The verification options must be documented on both the jwt and jwk middlewares.
forces.quote: Can you add a description for the `verification` option to the JSDoc for each middleware `jwk` and `jwt`?
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The feature is targeted for the 4.9.0 minor release, though it may ship as a patch release depending on timing.
consequences.quote: This is a new feature so that it can be included in the next minor release `4.9.0`. Since it may be a bit far and this feature is not a big change, we may release it as a patch release.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Issuer verification is opt-in and does not impact existing users who don't set the option.
consequences.quote: As this only verifies when the user has added an expected issuer, this isn't a breaking change and won't impact usage.
consequences.author: yolocat-dev

consequences.claim: The new VerifyOptions shape makes it straightforward to add validation for additional claims (sub, aud) in follow-up work.
consequences.quote: I could take a look at some other claims and add them accordingly.
consequences.author: yolocat-dev

alternatives.name: Add `issuer` as a new positional parameter to verify() alongside existing `alg` parameter
alternatives.rejection_reason: Adding claim validations as positional arguments would cause the signature to grow unboundedly as more claims (sub, aud, etc.) are added later; bundling them into a single VerifyOptions object scales better.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Do you think it will be necessary to verify `sub`, `aud`, and others in the future? If so, the number of arguments will increase accordingly. Therefore, I think it would be better to combine the options into a single object.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Use `join` for serve-static path resolution to fix Windows drive-letter loss and path-traversal via backslash
decisions.summary: Previous serve-static implementation on Windows converted paths like C:\Users\yusuke\ by stripping the drive letter, producing /Users/yusuke and causing unintended behavior. A new `join` option is introduced on serve-static middleware, the duplicated `pathResolve` option is removed, and a `defaultJoin` implementation is provided as fallback.

context.claim: Using a function such as join from node:path can resolve the path bug and simplify the code, making the existing pathResolve option unnecessary.
context.quote: If we use a function such as `join` exported by `path:node`, we can solve this problem and simplify the code. The `pathResolve` option is also unnecessary.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: A reviewer identified that an attacker inserting a backslash in the request URL could break path restrictions because the whole string would be joined with backslashes.
context.quote: Currently, if an attacker inserts `\` into the request string, the entire string will be joined with `\` , which may cause the path restriction to fail.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: The approach is inspired by a corresponding PR in the node-server repository.
context.quote: It is inspired by https://github.com/honojs/node-server/pull/261
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: Previous serve-static implementation on Windows converted paths like C:\Users\yusuke\ by stripping the drive letter, producing /Users/yusuke and causing unintended behavior.
context.quote: In previous implementations, paths starting with `C:\Users\yusuke\` on Windows were converted to `/Users/yusuke`. This caused unintended behavior because the drive name was lost.
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The reviewer approved the final simplified defaultJoin patch.
decision.quote: Thank you. I think it's good!
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: defaultJoin is scoped to posix-only behavior; Windows-path support in defaultJoin is intentionally dropped because runtime-specific adapters can use their runtime's join.
decision.quote: For the separators issue, I made `defaultJoin` support only Linux (posix), and it does not support Windows. This means if runtime-depends serve static-middleware (`adapter/bun/serve-static.ts` or `adapter/deno/serve-static.ts`) does not specify `path` option, it can't handle Windows paths. But, I think it's okay to cut Windows support because these runtime-dependent serve static-middleware can use `join` in the runtime API.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: For Deno, node:path's join is used (instead of implementing a separate solution).
decision.quote: For Deno, as you said, we can use `node:path`'s `join`. Updated: https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4291/commits/b3738f7241d281e4820893dc71ef6b217132e41f
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: A new `join` option is introduced on serve-static middleware, the duplicated `pathResolve` option is removed, and a `defaultJoin` implementation is provided as fallback.
decision.quote:
* Duplicated `pathResolve` option
* Added `join` option
* Simplified `src/middleware/serve-static/index.ts`
* Implemented `defaultJoin`. It is used if the `join` option is not specified
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Code style constraint: Hono has always used braces with if statements.
forces.quote: Until now, at hono, we have always used `{` with `if`, so I think it would be better to use it here as well.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Code-size minimization is a concern because defaultJoin is infrequently used.
forces.quote: As it stands, the frequency of defaultJoin usage is very low, so I think it's important to keep the code size small, so I think it would be better to do the following.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Security constraint: backslash injection in request strings must not be allowed to bypass path restrictions across OSes.
forces.quote:
When this app is run in a Linux environment, it will search for the following file.

```
static\test\test.txt
```

In a Windows environment, this file is located in the `static` directory, but in a Linux environment, it is located in the current directory as `static\test\test.txt`.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Fix must ship quickly without bumping major version — author chose a minor release.
forces.quote: Other behaviors remain unchanged. Additionally, unexpected path resolution issues should be resolved immediately. Release a minor version without changing the major version.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The path passed to onFound and onNotFound callbacks has slightly changed — the only behavioral change beyond the fix.
consequences.quote: I changed the test code, but only `path` passed to `onFound` and `onNotFound` has been slightly changed.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The previous index.html fallback behavior on Cloudflare Workers serve-static was removed because the adapter cannot determine whether a target path is a directory.
consequences.quote:
In the previous implementation, if the file was not found, it checked whether `index.html` existed. This PR, that process has been removed.

Since serve static for Cloudflare Workers is deprecated and not planned to be used, this change is acceptable.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Runtime-specific adapters (bun, deno) must supply a platform-aware join (via node:path) to get Windows path handling; defaultJoin will not handle Windows.
consequences.quote: This means if runtime-depends serve static-middleware (`adapter/bun/serve-static.ts` or `adapter/deno/serve-static.ts`) does not specify `path` option, it can't handle Windows paths.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Keep the existing `pathResolve` option
alternatives.rejection_reason: Using `join` from node:path makes pathResolve unnecessary and allows simplifying the code.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: If we use a function such as `join` exported by `path:node`, we can solve this problem and simplify the code. The `pathResolve` option is also unnecessary.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Use `@std/path` from JSR for Deno adapter
alternatives.rejection_reason: Preferred node:path because it is built into Deno.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: We can use [@std/path](https://jsr.io/@std/path/doc), but it's better to use `node:path` because it's built into Deno.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Avoid `node:` namespace modules in the Deno adapter (status-quo prior to this PR)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer argued that because implementing join is difficult, it is safer to depend on node:path even in Deno — which is consistent with already using node: modules in bun.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
I think the reason is probably because we haven't used modules in the "node:" namespace in this file before, but is that correct? (We have already used them in bun.)

If that is the case, as mentioned below, `join` processing is difficult, so I think it would be safer to depend on `nodo:path` even in Deno.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Have `defaultJoin` support Windows paths (detect drive letters / backslashes)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Author intentionally restricted defaultJoin to posix-only; runtime-specific adapters can bring their own Windows-capable join via the `path` option, and always using `/` as separator avoids backslash-based path-restriction bypass.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: For the separators issue, I made `defaultJoin` support only Linux (posix), and it does not support Windows.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Keep the more elaborate defaultJoin implementation with explicit empty-paths handling, absolute-path preservation, and `..` edge-case handling
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer proposed a smaller patch because defaultJoin is rarely used and code size matters; author accepted the simplification.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: As it stands, the frequency of defaultJoin usage is very low, so I think it's important to keep the code size small, so I think it would be better to do the following.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Add `parseResponse` util to smartly parse `hc`'s Response with type inference and structured errors
decisions.summary: Users have asked for a way to centralize common response-parsing logic (error checking and JSON parsing) for every `hc` client call. The PR adds an `hcParse` (later renamed `parseResponse`) util that automatically parses an `hc` fetch response, throwing a structured error if the response is not ok, and is type-safe.

our current solution is to wrap every client call with a custom method that checks for errors and converts to json, e.g.
