decisions.title: Add opt-in Sec-Fetch-Site based CSRF protection to the CSRF middleware
decisions.summary: Modern browsers support Fetch Metadata headers (Sec-Fetch-Site) that provide a more reliable way to detect cross-origin requests than Origin header checking alone. Add an opt-in `secFetchSite` option to the CSRF middleware that treats requests as allowed when either the Sec-Fetch-Site check or the Origin check passes, while keeping current origin-based behavior by default.

context.claim: Modern browsers support Fetch Metadata headers (Sec-Fetch-Site) that provide a more reliable way to detect cross-origin requests than Origin header checking alone.
context.quote: Modern browsers support [Fetch Metadata headers (Sec-Fetch-Site)](https://web.dev/articles/fetch-metadata) which provide a more reliable way to detect cross-origin requests than traditional Origin header checking alone.
context.author: meck93

context.claim: The current origin-based CSRF check does not itself pose a security issue, so the new mechanism need not replace it.
context.quote: However, since the current origin-based determination does not pose any security issues, I don't think this PR, which completely changes the behavior as an option, is a good approach.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: Using sec-fetch-site can simply and robustly expand the set of requests that are allowed beyond what pure origin-checking permits.
context.quote: If we use `sec-fetch-site` for determination, we can expand the scope of allowed requests in a simpler and more robust way than the current (on main branch) approach.
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Add an opt-in `secFetchSite` option to the CSRF middleware that treats requests as allowed when either the Sec-Fetch-Site check or the Origin check passes, while keeping current origin-based behavior by default.
decision.quote: refactor(csrf): replace useFetchMetadata with secFetchSite option for cleaner API
decision.author: meck93

decision.claim: Maintainers approved merging the opt-in design because the implementation is minimal and simple.
decision.quote:
Looks good to me! I like this PR since the implementation is minimal and simple.

This is a new feature, but just adding the option is slight. So, I'll merge this into main right now and include the next patch release.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The `secFetchSite` option values are typed to the valid spec values (`same-origin`, `same-site`, `none`, `cross-site`) for better DX and type safety.
decision.quote: feat(csrf): add test for blocking unknown secFetchSite values and enhance type safety
decision.author: meck93

forces.claim: Full backwards compatibility with existing origin-based CSRF protection is a requirement.
forces.quote: This enhancement adds opt-in support for Fetch Metadata based CSRF protection while maintaining full backwards compatibility.
forces.author: meck93

forces.claim: The CSRF middleware is scoped to reject simple CORS requests (as defined by MDN's 'simple requests'), which historically uses GET and HEAD as trusted methods.
forces.quote:
This middleware is intended to check for CSRF attacks and reject requests that match the following simple requests.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/CORS#simple_requests

With this background, we specified GET and HEAD as trusted methods.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Changes should not expand what the middleware rejects; in particular, cross-origin requests unrelated to CSRF attacks should not be newly blocked.
forces.quote: Furthermore, with the implementation in this PR, cross-origin requests that are not related to CSRF attacks are also targeted, which I think exceeds the checks that should be performed by this middleware.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Per the Fetch Metadata spec, servers should ignore unknown Sec-Fetch-Site values for forward compatibility, which constrains how strictly the type can be enforced.
forces.quote: Valid Sec-Fetch-Site values include "cross-site", "same-origin", "same-site", and "none". In order to support forward-compatibility with as-yet-unknown request types, servers SHOULD ignore this header if it contains an invalid value.
forces.author: meck93

consequences.claim: Unknown Sec-Fetch-Site values are explicitly blocked via a type guard, covered by a new test.
consequences.quote: feat(csrf): add test for blocking unknown secFetchSite values and enhance type safety
consequences.author: meck93

consequences.claim: Adding OPTIONS to the safe methods set is deferred to a separate future PR to avoid allowing requests that are currently rejected.
consequences.quote: I agree with the idea of changing this middleware to treat OPTIONS as a trusted method in the future. However, this change could result in requests that were previously rejected being allowed, so I would like to discuss this in a separate PR.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: If the Sec-Fetch-Site allowed values ever change in the spec, the `SecFetchSite` type will need to be updated.
consequences.quote: I guess we'd have to update the type if the allowed values ever change but this should be an easy fix at this point.
consequences.author: meck93

alternatives.name: Typing `secFetchSite` loosely as plain `string | string[]`
alternatives.rejection_reason: A narrow union type (`'same-origin' | 'same-site' | 'none' | 'cross-site'`) improves developer experience and enables editor autocompletion; adopted with an `isSecFetchSite` type guard to still block unknown values.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: The `string` should be only `same-origin`, `same-site`, `none`, or `cross-site`, right? If so, (this is a matter of test), you can define types like the following to improve the DX:
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Replace origin-based CSRF detection wholesale with Fetch Metadata (original PR design using `useFetchMetadata`)
alternatives.rejection_reason: The original design completely changed the middleware's behavior and would reject cross-origin requests unrelated to CSRF; since origin-based checking is not itself insecure, a less invasive opt-in was preferred.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: However, since the current origin-based determination does not pose any security issues, I don't think this PR, which completely changes the behavior as an option, is a good approach. Furthermore, with the implementation in this PR, cross-origin requests that are not related to CSRF attacks are also targeted, which I think exceeds the checks that should be performed by this middleware.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Alternative implementation proposed in PR #4355 (usualoma's sec-fetch-site based expansion of allowed requests)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Not rejected outright — the PR author agreed with the simpler opt-in direction from #4355 and adjusted this PR to match that approach rather than merging #4355.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
I agree with your idea and the proposal to make the change less fundamental and more of an opt-in approach. 

I'll adjust my PR accordingly.
alternatives.author: meck93

alternatives.name: Add OPTIONS to the set of safe HTTP methods (`isSafeMethodRe`) in this PR
alternatives.rejection_reason: Allowing OPTIONS as a trusted method could let through requests currently rejected by the middleware; the maintainer wanted this discussed and shipped as a separate PR.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
I would like to remove the addition of OPTIONS from this PR.

This middleware is intended to check for CSRF attacks and reject requests that match the following simple requests.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Add `customFetch` option to proxy helper for overriding global fetch
decisions.summary: The proxy helper used the global fetch with no way to override it, blocking use cases like tracing-instrumented fetch. Add a `customFetch` option on the proxy helper's second argument (ProxyRequestInit) to override the global fetch.

context.claim: A second user wanted override capability for logging the request/response of the internal fetch for debugging, since the request object is local to the helper.
context.quote: I have the same feature request, just for simply logging the request/response of this `fetch` call for easier debugging. Actually, logging the response is easy as it's almost exposed as the return value, but the request object is local inside this helper function and hard to see it from outside.
context.author: riywo

context.claim: The proxy helper used the global fetch with no way to override it, blocking use cases like tracing-instrumented fetch.
context.quote: Currently, `hono/proxy` uses the global `fetch,` and there is no way to override it.
context.author: karthik2804

context.claim: A concrete motivating use case was using `request.tracer.fetch` from @cloudflare/workers-honeycomb-logger so proxied requests appear in traces.
context.quote: I have an instance where I am using [`@cloudflare/workers-honeycome-logger`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cloudflare/workers-honeycomb-logger/v/0.3.6), and I’d like to use `request.tracer.fetch` so proxied requests are included in traces.
context.author: karthik2804

decision.claim: Add a `customFetch` option on the proxy helper's second argument (ProxyRequestInit) to override the global fetch.
decision.quote: This PR will add the option parameter `customFetch` to allow the user to override the global `fetch` in Proxy helper.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The final API places `customFetch` directly on the ProxyRequestInit options object rather than a third argument or a unique symbol key.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The signature was narrowed from `typeof fetch` to `(request: Request) => Promise<Response>` because it is only ever called with a Request.
decision.quote:
I think the type should be..

```ts
fetch: (request: Request) => Promise<Response>
```

..as it's only ever called with a `Request`.
decision.author: BarryThePenguin

forces.claim: The option needed to be ergonomic and avoid adding a third argument to the proxy function.
forces.quote: At least that way you could avoid adding a third argument?
forces.author: BarryThePenguin

forces.claim: The property name had to be distinct from any existing RequestInit field so it could live alongside normal request init options without collision.
forces.quote: Because `RequestInit` never has a property named `fetch`.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Ease of understanding for casual users was prioritized over more exotic API patterns.
forces.quote: Using a `unique symbol` like `oauth4webapi` is interesting, but for the casual user, using `customFetch` as a string property is easy to understand.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users can spread c.req alongside customFetch on the same options object.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: `customFetch` is optional; when absent, the global fetch is used.
consequences.quote: make it optional
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The custom fetch type is narrower than the platform `fetch` — it only accepts a Request and returns a Promise<Response>.
consequences.quote: customFetch: (request: Request) => Promise<Response>
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Third positional `options` argument (initial PR design with separate ProxyOptions)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Adding a third argument was considered awkward; reviewer suggested avoiding it, and the author agreed, moving the option into the existing second-argument init object.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: At least that way you could avoid adding a third argument?
alternatives.author: BarryThePenguin

alternatives.name: Unique-symbol key like oauth4webapi's `customFetch` symbol
alternatives.rejection_reason: Interesting but considered less approachable than a plain string property for casual users.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Using a `unique symbol` like `oauth4webapi` is interesting, but for the casual user, using `customFetch` as a string property is easy to understand.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Name the property `fetch` with type `typeof fetch`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Naming it `fetch` was deemed not entirely correct since the function is only ever called with a Request, not the full fetch signature; renamed to `customFetch` with a narrower type.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Even naming it `fetch` isn't entirely correct 🤔
alternatives.author: BarryThePenguin

alternatives.name: Use the existing RPC client custom fetch feature
alternatives.rejection_reason: That feature only applies to the RPC client, not the server-side proxy helper where the override is needed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think the custom fetch feature is for RPC client only. AFAIU, this request is on the server side here
alternatives.author: riywo

decisions.title: Expose request cloning as a standalone `cloneRawRequest` utility rather than modifying HonoRequest
decisions.summary: After Hono validators parse the body, the raw Request object is consumed and cannot be reused by external libraries like better-auth. The solution chosen is a standalone `cloneRawRequest` utility function, exported from `hono/request`, that handles both consumed and unconsumed bodies.

context.claim: After Hono validators parse the body, the raw Request object is consumed and cannot be reused by external libraries like better-auth.
context.quote:
After using Hono validators, the `raw` Request obejct gets consumed
during body parsing, making it unusable for external libraries like
`better-auth`.
context.author: kamaal111

context.claim: The original author's motivating use case is running validator middleware before invoking better-auth's handler (which requires a usable Request object) in the same handler.
context.quote: The main problem I have been facing is that I would like to run validating middleware before I enter my handler, and my handler body would invoke better-auth's handler
context.author: kamaal111

context.claim: The consumption originates in #cachedBody in HonoRequest, which calls parsing methods directly on the raw Request and consumes its stream.
context.quote:
The issue occurs in the `#cachedBody` method in `HonoRequest`. When parsing request
bodies (json, text, etc.), the method directly calls parsing methods on the raw Request
object, which consumes its body stream. Once consumed, the Request cannot be cloned or
reused by external libraries.
context.author: kamaal111

decision.claim: The maintainer confirmed naming/placement: export `cloneRawRequest` from `hono/request` (i.e. add it to `src/request.ts`) rather than creating a new helper file.
decision.quote: Ah, `hono/request` is good! If so, it may be natural to add `cloneRawRequest` to `src/request.ts`. We don't need to create `src/helper/request.ts` or `src/helper/request-helper.ts`. Keeping the structure simple is important.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Final approval and merge decision by maintainer into `next` for v4.10.0.
decision.quote:
Let's go with this!

I'll merge this into the `next` branch for the next minor release `v4.10.0`
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The solution chosen is a standalone `cloneRawRequest` utility function, exported from `hono/request`, that handles both consumed and unconsumed bodies.
decision.quote:
Adding a utility function to clone HonoRequest's underlying raw Request
object, handling both consumed and unconsumed request bodies.
decision.author: kamaal111

decision.claim: Maintainer agreed a helper is preferable to modifying HonoRequest and proposed adopting usualoma's proposed helper shape.
decision.quote: Making a helper is nice to keep the HonoRequest clean.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Cloning the request inside HonoRequest's hot path would introduce significant performance degradation, which the maintainer demonstrated with a bombardier benchmark.
forces.quote: Why I didn't implement cloning the request is that it will introduce significant performance degradation.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: A `raw` getter returning a reconstructed Request cannot work because body caches are stored as Promises and a getter cannot be async.
forces.quote: The problem is that cached values are `Promise`. And the `raw` getter can not be an `async` (right?).
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: `c.req.raw` is semantically expected to return the original Request object, so replacing it with a newly constructed Request would violate that contract.
forces.quote: I think `c.req.raw` is expected to return the “original object”. Returning something newly created with `new Request()` might not be ideal.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The scenario of consuming the body twice is relatively rare, which argues for an opt-in utility rather than baked-in behavior.
forces.quote: the scenario of “consuming the body with `c.req` and then consuming it again with `c.req.raw`” is relatively rare. Therefore, adding a utility method might be preferable to increasing the `HonoRequest` code.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The core HonoRequest object is expected to remain simple and small; growing its logic is undesirable.
forces.quote: But it's not good that the logic/code has increased. The core object, `HonoRequest`, should be simple and small.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The clone must preserve all RequestInit properties (mode, credentials, cache, etc.), not just method/headers/body, to avoid unexpected behavior for users.
forces.quote: Should we copy other properties, such as `credentials`, `mode`, and `single`?
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Shipped in the v4.10.0 minor release via the `next` branch.
consequences.quote: I'll merge this into the `next` branch for the next minor release `v4.10.0`
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Documentation for the utility will live on the HonoRequest page on hono.dev.
consequences.quote: Additionally, we can document the usage of `cloneRawRequest` on the `HonoRequest` page (https://hono.dev/docs/api/request) on the website.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The utility returns a Promise<Request>, requiring callers to `await` it even when the body is not consumed.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users opt in to cloning only when needed, keeping HonoRequest's hot path unchanged and avoiding performance regressions for the common case.
consequences.quote: We should find a good way to resolve the problem without performance delegation.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Replace `raw` with a getter that returns a freshly reconstructed Request when the body has been consumed
alternatives.rejection_reason: A getter cannot be async, but cached body values are stored as Promises, so the getter cannot synchronously materialize the body needed to build a new Request. Also violates the semantic expectation that `c.req.raw` returns the original object.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think adding a raw getter is good, but how to create a new Request object if it has been consumed? The problem is that cached values are `Promise`. And the `raw` getter can not be an `async` (right?).
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Cache resolved body values (instead of Promises) in HonoRequest so that a sync `raw` getter could work (explored in PR #4425)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Superseded by usualoma's suggestion to add an external utility function instead, which keeps HonoRequest unchanged; maintainer agreed a helper is preferable.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Interrupting is no problem! Thank you for the idea. Making a helper is nice to keep the HonoRequest clean. I may make it without the problem @kamaal111 mentioned. I'll work on it later.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: User-space workaround: rebuild a Request outside Hono by reading `c.req.json()`/headers/method before calling the external library
alternatives.rejection_reason: Does not solve the case where validator middleware has already consumed the body before the handler runs, so the raw Request cannot be cloned externally.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: So for this cloning it outside of HonoRequest would not work, because the body has already been consumed before I get access to the raw Request object in my handler.
alternatives.author: kamaal111

alternatives.name: Make better-auth (and similar libraries) work with an already-consumed Request object
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered only a short-term fix; other future third-party integrations would likely have the same need, so a reusable solution in Hono is preferable.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Alternatively we can also make better-auth work with a "consumed" Request object, but this is a very short term solution as there might be other third party integrations in the future that would like to use the Request object somehow.
alternatives.author: kamaal111

alternatives.name: Place `cloneRawRequest` in a new helper module such as `hono/request-helper` or `hono/clone`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected in favor of exporting from the existing `hono/request` alongside `HonoRequest`, avoiding creation of a new helper file and keeping the package structure simple.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ah, `hono/request` is good! If so, it may be natural to add `cloneRawRequest` to `src/request.ts`. We don't need to create `src/helper/request.ts` or `src/helper/request-helper.ts`. Keeping the structure simple is important.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Clone the raw Request inside HonoRequest's body-parsing path (the PR's original approach)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer's benchmark showed it introduced significant performance degradation on a simple POST JSON route, which is unacceptable for the core request path.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Why I didn't implement cloning the request is that it will introduce significant performance degradation.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Copy only method/headers/body when reconstructing the Request
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would drop other RequestInit properties (mode, credentials, cache, redirect, referrer, referrerPolicy, integrity, keepalive), risking unexpected behavior for users who rely on those fields.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
You are right, it might cause unexpected behavior if the user is expecting these properties.
I have explicitly declared and copied over all `RequestInit` properties except for `priority` and `window`
alternatives.author: kamaal111

alternatives.name: Add a `new()` / reconstruction method on HonoRequest that builds a fresh Request from cached body data
alternatives.rejection_reason: Implementation worked but grew HonoRequest's logic/code; maintainer wants the core object to remain simple and small.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Adding a `new()` function is a great idea that I also think is similar! And the implementation seems to be fine. But it's not good that the logic/code has increased. The core object, `HonoRequest`, should be simple and small.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Propagate middleware response types through RPC by extending MiddlewareHandler with a fourth type parameter
decisions.summary: Hono's RPC mode did not expose middleware responses to the client, so errors returned from middleware (e.g., auth failures) were invisible to the type system. MiddlewareHandler gains a fourth type parameter to carry middleware response types, and validator middlewares (zod, valibot) are adjusted to propagate those types through RPC.

context.claim: Hono's RPC mode did not expose middleware responses to the client, so errors returned from middleware (e.g., auth failures) were invisible to the type system.
context.quote: Currently, the RPC mode does not support middleware response. The client can't handle the response from the middleware:
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: Users reported the missing middleware response typing broke practical RPC use cases such as authentication middleware.
context.quote: currently i have a authentication middleware but without this feature I am having to authenticate at every request to get typings correctly.
context.author: dvrfluxchat

context.claim: Previous attempts at implementing this (PRs #3292 and #4252) stalled because the type definitions became too complex to finish.
context.quote: I've tried to create both PRs while struggling with TypeScript types! I think the base ideas are good, but the type definitions became too complex and can not be finished.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: This PR builds on the earlier draft PR #4252, reusing its test suite.
context.quote: It's somewhat based off this draft PR: #4252 (I copied majority of tests from there, thanks!)
context.author: slawekkolodziej

decision.claim: A second reviewer confirmed merging was acceptable.
decision.quote: My bad for being late. I think you can merge it.
decision.author: m-shaka

decision.claim: MiddlewareHandler gains a fourth type parameter to carry middleware response types, and validator middlewares (zod, valibot) are adjusted to propagate those types through RPC.
decision.quote: After some more changes in validator middlewares (I already adjusted `@hono/zod-validator` and `@hono/valibot-validator`) it gives proper error codes through RPC.
decision.author: slawekkolodziej

decision.claim: The maintainer decided to accept the PR and merge it into the `next` branch for the v4.10.0 minor release.
decision.quote: I'll merge this into [the `next` branch](https://github.com/honojs/hono/tree/next) for the next minor release `v4.10.0`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The PR was approved with an LGTM review by the maintainer.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Making middleware response behavior work as expected (correctness / DX) is a core goal of the change.
forces.quote: making behavior expected is also important for achieving better DX.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: TypeScript type-inference performance is a competing force: the change must be weighed against slower compilation and potential editor hangs.
forces.quote: It's a common problem with the performance of type inference. If the project becomes big, the PRC will be super slow, and the editor may hang.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Backwards compatibility concerns were raised previously: exposing middleware response types can cause type errors in many existing Hono RPC projects.
forces.quote: However, merging this may cause type errors in many projects currently using Hono's RPC, so I think this should be considered carefully.
forces.author: kosei28

consequences.claim: TypeScript instantiations increase by ~100,074 (+18.6%), and memory usage and check time rise on the sample tsc --diagnostics run.
consequences.quote: The `Instantiations` will be `538082` to `638156`, this means, +100,074 +18.6% increase. I think this is not small.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The maintainer accepted the performance degradation, planning to address perf via other means later.
consequences.quote: I think we can accept this PR, though bringing the performance degradation since making consistency is significant, and we may resolve the performance issue in other ways.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: A follow-up adjustment to `app.use` types is needed so typed middleware can be passed without type errors; this was planned and landed in a subsequent commit.
consequences.quote: Without it `app.use` is complaining about invalid type when you try to pass a 'typed middleware' to it. I should be able to adjust this soon.
consequences.author: slawekkolodziej

consequences.claim: Validator middlewares (zod, valibot) require updates to propagate the new middleware response types; a follow-up PR for those will be created.
consequences.quote: I'll create another PR for zod & valibot middlewares to have proper type inference with those two (I already have working code locally). I think those two can serve as good starting point for other middleware's maintainers.
consequences.author: slawekkolodziej

consequences.claim: The second reviewer judged the instantiation increase to be acceptable relative to past changes.
consequences.quote: Increase of instantiations isn't a disaster and way much better compared to the past, for example in #3439
consequences.author: m-shaka

alternatives.name: Keep the performance by not merging (preserve current behavior without middleware response types)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer framed this as an explicit choice between making behavior correct and keeping performance, and decided correctness/DX consistency was more significant; performance could be addressed later by other means.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
So, we should choose.

1. Make them behave as expected
2. Keep the performance

Both are important. ... I think we can accept this PR, though bringing the performance degradation since making consistency is significant, and we may resolve the performance issue in other ways.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Prior draft PR #4252 (NamesMT's middleware response type inference attempt)
alternatives.rejection_reason: The earlier attempt stalled because its type definitions became too complex to finish; the current PR reuses its tests but supersedes it with a simpler implementation.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I've tried to create both PRs while struggling with TypeScript types! I think the base ideas are good, but the type definitions became too complex and can not be finished. If you have any idea, please steal the code and create another PR.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: PR #3292 — client-side `DefaultResponse` type escape hatch
alternatives.rejection_reason: Not rejected outright but treated as complementary rather than a substitute: #3292 adds a client-side escape hatch, while this PR provides real middleware type inference.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
#3292 latest state is about adding a `DefaultResponse` type "escape hatch" that the user can define on the client side, to populate type context for any route.
* #4252 is adding real middleware types infer, HUGE update.

The two PRs is complementary.
alternatives.author: NamesMT

alternatives.name: User-space `compose` utility wrapping middleware with handlers
alternatives.rejection_reason: Raised by a user in the linked issue as a workaround while waiting for the feature; no maintainer adopted it as a solution and the PR instead implemented first-class type inference.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Any way to write a compose util to solve this until this is implemented?
```ts
app.get("/", compose(auth(), () => ...))
```
alternatives.author: motherthestate

decisions.title: Add a defaultPlugin to SSG that defines recommended behavior (skip redirects and non-200 responses)
decisions.summary: The PR originated from a discussion in issue #4389 about recommended SSG default behavior. A defaultPlugin is added to SSG that encodes recommended behavior and is applied automatically when no plugins option is provided.

context.claim: Popular static hosting providers support custom 404 pages, so SSG should not generate output for such routes by default.
context.quote: Popular static site hosting services support custom 404 pages, so I think that, like redirects, it is better for SSGs not to generate anything by default.
context.author: 3w36zj6

context.claim: c.notFound() generates plaintext by default, which conflicts with hosting providers that support custom 404 pages.
context.quote: By the way, I noticed that `c.notFound()` also generates plaintext.
context.author: 3w36zj6

context.claim: The PR originated from a discussion in issue #4389 about recommended SSG default behavior.
context.quote: cf. https://github.com/honojs/hono/issues/4389#issuecomment-3248655086
context.author: 3w36zj6

decision.claim: The defaultPlugin is injected as the default value when options.plugins is omitted, so users get the default behavior implicitly.
decision.quote: Yes, I think so. Users must have the default behavior if they do not set anything.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Maintainer agreed to keep the defaultPlugin abstraction rather than inlining redirect handling into toSSG.
decision.quote: Okay. Let's go with keeping the current implementation using `defaultPlugin`!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The defaultPlugin was extended to skip all non-200 responses, not only redirects.
decision.quote: fix(ssg): update default plugin to skip non-200 responses
decision.author: 3w36zj6

decision.claim: Final approval to merge was given by yusukebe.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: A defaultPlugin is added to SSG that encodes recommended behavior and is applied automatically when no plugins option is provided.
decision.quote: feat(ssg): add default plugin that defines the recommended behavior
decision.author: 3w36zj6

forces.claim: Users must receive the recommended behavior even when they do not configure plugins explicitly.
forces.quote: Users must have the default behavior if they do not set anything.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Keeping default behavior in a plugin preserves a minimal toSSG implementation while enabling future extensibility, API consistency, and testability.
forces.quote: by separating the default behavior as a plugin, I believe we can keep the implementation of `toSSG` minimal while also gaining various advantages such as future extensibility, API consistency, and ease of testing.
forces.author: 3w36zj6

forces.claim: 3xx redirects and 4xx/5xx errors are not meaningful files to generate for a static site; only 200 OK responses should be materialized.
forces.quote: 4xx (like `404`) and 5xx (like `500`) are errors, so there's no need to output files. Additionally, 3xx are redirects, so they're unnecessary. For 2xx, I don't think we need anything other than `200`, like `201 Created`, `202 Accepted`, or `204 No Content`.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Log output is intentionally not included in the default plugin; users can implement their own hooks if they need logging, and documentation could showcase this as an SSG plugin use case.
consequences.quote: it would be a good idea to introduce log output as a use case in the documentation for SSG plugins.
consequences.author: 3w36zj6

consequences.claim: When plugins is not set, SSG now applies defaultPlugin, skipping non-200 responses (including redirects and 404s).
consequences.quote: fix(ssg): set default plugin in options if none provided
consequences.author: 3w36zj6

consequences.claim: The defaultPlugin is marked experimental, signaling that its API may change.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Custom 404 pages should be defined with a 200 status; hosting providers will assign the 404 status at serve time.
consequences.quote: if we define this page with a 200 status, I believe there is no issue. When accessing the 404 page directly in a production environment, the hosting service will automatically assign a 404 status.
consequences.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Inline the redirect-skipping logic directly in toSSG (no defaultPlugin abstraction)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected in favor of keeping the plugin abstraction because separating default behavior as a plugin keeps toSSG minimal and provides future extensibility, API consistency, and ease of testing.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: by separating the default behavior as a plugin, I believe we can keep the implementation of `toSSG` minimal while also gaining various advantages such as future extensibility, API consistency, and ease of testing.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Include log output in the defaultPlugin
alternatives.rejection_reason: No log format is obviously suitable as a standard; Vite already outputs logs when using @hono/vite-ssg; users can implement their own hooks for logging if needed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I can't think of a log format that would be suitable as a standard. Also, since Vite outputs logs when using @hono/vite-ssg, it's probably better not to include log output by default. If logging is needed, users can implement their own hooks to suit their particular use case.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Skip only redirects (3xx) and 404 responses in the defaultPlugin
alternatives.rejection_reason: Broadened to skip all non-200 responses because 4xx and 5xx are errors that shouldn't produce files, 3xx are unnecessary redirects, and other 2xx codes (201, 202, 204) are not meaningful to materialize either.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
after reconsidering, perhaps, we have to handle only a `200 OK` response.

4xx (like `404`) and 5xx (like `500`) are errors, so there's no need to output files. Additionally, 3xx are redirects, so they're unnecessary. For 2xx, I don't think we need anything other than `200`, like `201 Created`, `202 Accepted`, or `204 No Content`.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Require users to pass plugins explicitly (no implicit default)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected because users must get the recommended default behavior when they do not configure anything.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Users must have the default behavior if they do not set anything.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Use protected methods instead of computed properties in PreparedRegExpRouter to enable tree shaking
decisions.summary: Computed properties in the module caused bundlers to assume possible side effects, producing larger bundled output. Replace computed properties with protected methods to enable tree shaking.

context.claim: Computed properties in the module caused bundlers to assume possible side effects, producing larger bundled output.
context.quote: The presence of computed properties led to the determination that side effects were possible, resulting in larger bundled code.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: Bundling a simple import of PreparedRegExpRouter on main produced roughly 15,633 bytes of output.
context.quote:
$ npx esbuild --bundle prr.ts | wc
     464    1619   15633
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: On the refactor branch, the same bundling command produced roughly 2,790 bytes.
context.quote:
$ npx esbuild --bundle import.ts | wc
      92     262    2790
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Replace computed properties with protected methods to enable tree shaking.
decision.quote: refactor: use protected methods instead of computed properties to allow `tree shaking`
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the change after confirming the bundle-size reduction.
decision.quote: I confirmed this change affects the new project and reduces the file size without RegExpRouter. Thanks!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Maintainer gave final approval with LGTM.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Bundlers treat computed properties as potentially side-effecting, preventing dead-code elimination.
forces.quote: The presence of computed properties led to the determination that side effects were possible, resulting in larger bundled code.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: PreparedRegExpRouter uses RegExpRouter as a base class, so the import cannot simply be type-only.
forces.quote: We're using it as a base class here!
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Overall bundle size of the project decreased by 716 bytes (from 19,051B to 18,335B).
consequences.author: github-actions

consequences.claim: HTTP benchmark shows a small performance regression of roughly 0.79% on average.
consequences.author: github-actions

consequences.claim: If bundling results are still not optimal, files could be further split to minimize impact.
consequences.quote: If bundling doesn't produce the intended result, I think it's possible to separate the files to minimize the impact.
consequences.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Convert RegExpRouter import to a type-only import
alternatives.rejection_reason: RegExpRouter is used as a base class, not just as a type, so the import cannot be made type-only.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: We're using it as a base class here!
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Make RFC 9110 Connection header processing opt-in via strictConnectionProcessing to prevent hop-by-hop header injection
decisions.summary: The existing proxy helper referenced the obsolete RFC 2616 for hop-by-hop header handling. Added a strictConnectionProcessing option (defaulting to false) that gates RFC 9110 Connection-header processing; the default ignores the Connection header to avoid injection attacks.

context.claim: The existing proxy helper referenced the obsolete RFC 2616 for hop-by-hop header handling.
context.quote: The code references [RFC 2616](https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc2616), which has been obsoleted by RFC 7230 (2014) and then RFC 9110 (2022).
context.author: sugar-cat7

context.claim: RFC 9110 requires intermediaries to parse the Connection header and remove any headers/trailers named by connection-options.
context.quote: Intermediaries MUST parse a received Connection header field before a message is forwarded and, for each connection-option in this field, remove any header or trailer field(s) from the message with the same name as the connection-option
context.author: sugar-cat7

context.claim: Unconditionally honoring the Connection header would let clients cause removal of sensitive headers like X-Forwarded-For or X-Real-IP (hop-by-hop header injection).
context.quote:
However, if headers like the following can be specified in the connection value and deleted, it seems this could lead to issues classified as "Hop-by-Hop Header Injection".
* X-Forwarded-For
* X-Real-IP
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Invalid header handling uses HTTPException per reviewer guidance rather than returning a Response with status 400 directly.
decision.quote: Sorry for not explaining earlier, but I want to throw an `HTTPException`.
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Final merge approved and released as a minor bump.
decision.quote: Thanks! Merging and releasing a new version (it's a minor bump!).
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Added a strictConnectionProcessing option (defaulting to false) that gates RFC 9110 Connection-header processing; the default ignores the Connection header to avoid injection attacks.
decision.quote:
I've added a `strictConnectionProcessing` option (default: `false`):
- **Default behavior**: Ignores the Connection header entirely to prevent injection attacks
- **Strict mode** (`true`): Processes Connection header per RFC 9110 (use only in trusted environments)
decision.author: sugar-cat7

decision.claim: Reviewer confirmed strictConnectionProcessing is an acceptable API shape.
decision.quote: I believe `strictConnectionProcessing` should be fine!
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: Fixed 'trailers' → 'trailer' in hopByHopHeaders and added the missing 'upgrade' header to align with RFC 9110.
decision.quote: Changed `'trailers'` to `'trailer'` in `hopByHopHeaders` array
decision.author: sugar-cat7

decision.claim: Added 'upgrade' to hopByHopHeaders per RFC 9110 Section 7.6.1.
decision.quote:
Added missing `'upgrade'` header
  - Required per [RFC 9110 Section 7.6.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9110#section-7.6.1)
decision.author: sugar-cat7

decision.claim: Invalid Connection-header tokens validated per RFC 9110 §5.6.2 cause a 400/HTTPException rather than an Internal Server Error.
decision.quote: * Added header (token) validation based on RFC 9110 Section 5.6.2, returning 400 on validation failure.
decision.author: sugar-cat7

forces.claim: RFC 9110 compliance: intermediaries MUST remove headers named in a received Connection field.
forces.quote: Intermediaries MUST parse a received Connection header field before a message is forwarded and, for each connection-option in this field, remove any header or trailer field(s) from the message with the same name as the connection-option
forces.author: sugar-cat7

forces.claim: Security: naive compliance enables hop-by-hop header injection of trust-establishing headers like X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP.
forces.quote: it seems this could lead to issues classified as "Hop-by-Hop Header Injection".
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The opt-in should be used only in trusted environments.
forces.quote: Since this option is mainly used in trusted environments, we generally don't need to worry about invalid values.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Robustness: malformed Connection header values must not produce an Internal Server Error.
forces.quote: When `strictConnectionProcessing` is enabled, the following request results in an "Internal Server Error".
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Default proxy behavior remains non-compliant with the RFC 9110 MUST, in favor of security; strict compliance is opt-in.
consequences.quote:
- **Default behavior**: Ignores the Connection header entirely to prevent injection attacks
- **Strict mode** (`true`): Processes Connection header per RFC 9110 (use only in trusted environments)
consequences.author: sugar-cat7

consequences.claim: With strict mode enabled, invalid Connection tokens cause a 400 response via HTTPException instead of a 500.
consequences.quote: * Added header (token) validation based on RFC 9110 Section 5.6.2, returning 400 on validation failure.
consequences.author: sugar-cat7

consequences.claim: After switching to throwing, connection-header validation logic was consolidated back into buildRequestInitFromRequest.
consequences.quote: Reverted to initial implementation and consolidated logic into buildRequestInitFromRequest since errors are now thrown.
consequences.author: sugar-cat7

consequences.claim: Released as a minor version bump rather than a patch, reflecting the new public option.
consequences.quote: Merging and releasing a new version (it's a minor bump!).
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Always process the Connection header per RFC 9110 (no opt-in flag)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Would allow clients to inject connection-options that cause the proxy to strip security-relevant headers like X-Forwarded-For and X-Real-IP; the reviewer insisted it should only be enabled explicitly.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: While it's desirable to have functionality that respects the "Connection" header, I feel it should only be enabled explicitly as an option.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: On invalid Connection header, ignore it (skip processing) rather than error
alternatives.rejection_reason: Raised by the reviewer as one of two acceptable options, but the author chose the 400-error path instead (later refined to throw HTTPException).
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: In those cases, I think we should either return a 400 error or just ignore it.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Return a plain `new Response(..., { status: 400 })` for invalid Connection tokens
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer preferred throwing an HTTPException (consistent with validator.ts) over constructing a Response directly; author changed the implementation accordingly.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Sorry for not explaining earlier, but I want to throw an `HTTPException`.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Lowercase connection-option names before calling headers.delete()
alternatives.rejection_reason: Unnecessary because Headers.delete() already normalizes header names; `.toLowerCase()` was removed on reviewer suggestion.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: The header name is normalized within `headers.delete()`, so I believe `.toLowerCase()` is unnecessary.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Keep 'trailers' (plural) in the hop-by-hop headers list per RFC 2616
alternatives.rejection_reason: RFC 2616's 'Trailers' was corrected via Errata 4522 to 'Trailer', and RFCs 7230/9110 use the singular form.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: RFC 2616 originally listed "Trailers" but this was an error, corrected in [RFC 2616 Errata ID 4522](https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid4522) (Status: Verified)
alternatives.author: sugar-cat7

decisions.title: Extend bearerAuth with configurable WWW-Authenticate header and granular error messages for MCP compatibility
decisions.summary: The bearerAuth middleware needed an option to customize the WWW-Authenticate header value so Hono can comply with MCP Auth specs that require embedding resource_metadata. Scope was narrowed to only updating Bearer Auth middleware, preventing breaking changes, and deferring JWT/JWK changes to a later PR.

context.claim: The bearerAuth middleware needed an option to customize the WWW-Authenticate header value so Hono can comply with MCP Auth specs that require embedding resource_metadata.
context.quote: Add Options to change `"WWW-Authenticate"` header value in `bearerAuth`
context.author: MathurAditya724

context.claim: These improvements are motivated by easier integration with hono/mcp and compliance with MCP Auth specs.
context.quote: These improvements will make it easy to use them with `hono/mcp`, further simplifying MCP Auth in hono. Changes around `"WWW-Authenticate"` will help us to comply with MCP Auth Specs
context.author: MathurAditya724

context.claim: The author acknowledged that extending the auth middlewares would introduce breaking changes, which is why opinions were solicited first.
context.quote: This will introduce breaking changes to the Auth Middlewares, that's why wanted everyones opinion on this
context.author: MathurAditya724

decision.claim: Scope was narrowed to only updating Bearer Auth middleware, preventing breaking changes, and deferring JWT/JWK changes to a later PR.
decision.quote:
To process developing `@hono/mcp`, how about going with the following?

* Update only Bearer Auth middleware.
* Prevent breaking changes.

After it is done, let's release the patch version of `hono` and use it in `@hono/mcp`, so you don't need to copy the code.

We may need to apply similar changes to JWT/JWK, but we can do them later.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Author agreed to limit the PR to bearerAuth and split JWT/JWK changes into a separate future PR.
decision.quote:
Okay, I will update the branch 👍🏼 

I will create another PR for the JWT/JWK changes which we can merge later
decision.author: MathurAditya724

decision.claim: The reviewer ultimately accepted the author's nested-options approach (with @deprecated on old flat props) over his own verbose alternative, in order to reduce verbosity going forward.
decision.quote: I've thought this through and concluded that your changes are acceptable. [My suggestion](https://github.com/honojs/hono/pull/4485/files#r2480824650) does not introduce "deprecated"s, but it's better to reduce verbosity for the future.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The PR was approved and merged by yusukebe after a JSDoc fix.
decision.quote: I updated to fix JSDoc and it looks good. I'll merge this now and release a new patch version including this change.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The MCP Auth specification requires a specific WWW-Authenticate header format including a resource_metadata URL pointing to the protected-resource metadata endpoint.
forces.author: MathurAditya724

forces.claim: Backward compatibility was a hard requirement — the old props needed to remain functional.
forces.quote: Prevent breaking changes.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: A mitigation for the breaking-change concern was to keep old props and mark them with @deprecated JSDoc so they can be removed later.
forces.quote: we can also prevent this by keeping the old prop and adding a deprecated marker in JSDocs, so that in the future release we can remove that. And the old prop will be merged with the new on inside the function. This will only be needing for `bearerAuth` middleware
forces.author: MathurAditya724

forces.claim: Schedule pressure: the author wanted to unblock the hono/mcp package release quickly.
forces.quote: Hey, I really wanna push the `hono/mcp` package today, so if you feel `noAuthenticationHeaderWwwAuthenticateHeader` is a better approach I am happy to do it. Cause I think mostly it's just gonna be hono/mcp which uses this
forces.author: MathurAditya724

consequences.claim: The chosen design introduces @deprecated markers on the old flat message props, signaling future removal.
consequences.quote: @deprecated Use noAuthenticationHeader.message instead
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: A patch release of hono will be published containing these changes so hono/mcp can consume them without copying the bearerAuth source.
consequences.quote: After it is done, let's release the patch version of `hono` and use it in `@hono/mcp`, so you don't need to copy the code.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Analogous changes to JWT/JWK middleware are deferred to a later PR.
consequences.quote: We may need to apply similar changes to JWT/JWK, but we can do them later.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Introduce breaking changes by replacing the old flat option props outright
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected to preserve backward compatibility; old props were kept and marked @deprecated instead of being removed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Prevent breaking changes.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Add a single flat `noAuthenticationHeaderWwwAuthenticateHeader` option alongside existing flat message props (no deprecations, no nested options object)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer proposed this verbose, deprecation-free approach but ultimately accepted the author's nested-options design because reducing verbosity for the future was judged more valuable than avoiding @deprecated markers.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: My suggestion does not introduce "deprecated"s, but it's better to reduce verbosity for the future.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Copy the bearerAuth source file into the @hono/mcp package instead of upstreaming changes
alternatives.rejection_reason: The author had initially copied the whole bearerAuth file into the @hono/mcp PR; the maintainer preferred upstreaming the changes and releasing a hono patch so hono/mcp can depend on it directly rather than duplicate code.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: After it is done, let's release the patch version of `hono` and use it in `@hono/mcp`, so you don't need to copy the code.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Include JWT and JWK middleware changes in the same PR
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer asked to narrow scope to only bearerAuth to unblock hono/mcp quickly; JWT/JWK would be handled in a follow-up PR.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
Update only Bearer Auth middleware.

* Prevent breaking changes.

After it is done, let's release the patch version of `hono` and use it in `@hono/mcp`, so you don't need to copy the code.

We may need to apply similar changes to JWT/JWK, but we can do them later.

decisions.title: Make Hono client's $url return the exact URL type via new TypedURL generic
decisions.summary: The author wanted to reuse typed keys from SWR in other parts of the codebase, but $url did not preserve type information. Introduce a new TypedURL type and propagate generics through the client so $url returns a URL string typed with protocol, host, port, path, and query.

context.claim: The author wanted to reuse typed keys from SWR in other parts of the codebase, but $url did not preserve type information.
context.quote: I wanted to add types so that keys used with SWR would be easy to reuse in other parts of the codebase. However, $url did not preserve that type information, so I created this PR.
context.author: miyaji255

decision.claim: Maintainer reclassified the change from fix to feat, confirming it's a new feature rather than a bug fix.
decision.quote: Anyway, this change is not a "fix", but a "feat". It's a new feature.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Introduce a new TypedURL type and propagate generics through the client so $url returns a URL string typed with protocol, host, port, path, and query.
decision.quote: The main improvement is the introduction of a new `TypedURL` type, which allows the client to generate URLs with strongly-typed protocol, host, port, path, and query parameters based on the application's route schema and the provided base URL.
decision.author: miyaji255

decision.claim: The hc function requires the base URL to be passed as an explicit second generic argument (e.g. hc<typeof app, 'http://localhost'>).
decision.quote: const client = hc<typeof app, 'http://localhost'>('http://localhost')
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the PR as a new feature to ship in the next minor release.
decision.quote: This is awesome! We can include this feature in the next minor release. So, I'll merge when preparing to release it.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: URL generation needed to be accurate and type-checked at compile time.
forces.quote: ensuring that URL generation is accurate and type-checked at compile time.
forces.author: miyaji255

forces.claim: esbuild v0.15.18 doesn't support const type parameters, forcing an esbuild upgrade.
forces.quote: esbuild v0.15.18 doesn't support const type parameters, so I updated esbuild.
forces.author: miyaji255

forces.claim: TypeScript requires all other generics to be specified once any one generic is specified, constraining the API shape.
forces.quote: TypeScript requires you to specify all other generics if you specify one generic.
forces.author: miyaji255

consequences.claim: Users must pass the base URL twice — once as a type argument and once as a runtime argument — to get the precise URL type.
consequences.quote: const client = hc<typeof app, 'http://localhost'>('http://localhost')
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: A future helper function (e.g. createHc / hcStrict) using curried generics may be added later to avoid the duplicate specification, but is explicitly deferred out of this PR.
consequences.quote: Anyway, first, we will not include the function in this PR.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Several new generics and utility types are introduced throughout the client to propagate the URL type information.
consequences.quote: Several generics and utility types are introduced to propagate this type information throughout the client
consequences.author: miyaji255

alternatives.name: Single-argument hc() that infers the URL type from the runtime string without a second generic
alternatives.rejection_reason: TypeScript requires all other generics to be specified when one is explicitly provided, so a single-argument form can't carry a literal URL type through without additional API changes. Solving it would require a curried-generics helper, which the maintainer agreed to defer out of this PR.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: TypeScript requires you to specify all other generics if you specify one generic. If you want to solve this problem, you can solve it by currying generics like this.
alternatives.author: miyaji255

alternatives.name: Curried-generics helper (hcStrict/createHc) shipped in this PR to avoid the duplicate URL specification
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer agreed it's a good idea but explicitly chose not to include such a function in this PR, deferring it to future work.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Anyway, first, we will not include the function in this PR.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Enhance NotFoundHandler to support a user-extendable NotFoundResponse type via module augmentation
decisions.summary: The existing NotFoundHandler was typed to return a plain Response, offering no way to propagate the not-found response shape to the Hono RPC client. Introduce a NotFoundResponse interface that users can extend via TypeScript module augmentation to customize the c.notFound() response type.

context.claim: The existing NotFoundHandler was typed to return a plain Response, offering no way to propagate the not-found response shape to the Hono RPC client.
context.quote: -export type NotFoundHandler<E extends Env = any> = (c: Context<E>) => Response | Promise<Response>
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The author wanted users to be able to type the notFound response for consumption by the Hono client.
context.quote: This is how I'm planning to type the `notFound` response for the client, using module augmentation.
context.author: miyaji255

decision.claim: Introduce a NotFoundResponse interface that users can extend via TypeScript module augmentation to customize the c.notFound() response type.
decision.quote: I add a `NotFoundResponse` interface and support custom response types by allowing developers to extend `NotFoundResponse` for their own use cases.
decision.author: miyaji255

decision.claim: Maintainer approved the approach and scheduled it for the next minor release.
decision.quote: Looks good to me! I'll include this feature in the next minor version. I'll merge later.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: PR was approved with an LGTM review.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The custom not-found type must flow through to Hono's RPC client type inference (hc), so that client.route.$get() knows the 404 body shape.
forces.author: miyaji255

forces.claim: c.notFound() must return the augmented NotFoundResponse type rather than a plain Response to make the customization actually take effect.
forces.quote:
-  notFound = (): Response | Promise<Response> => {
+  notFound = (): ReturnType<NotFoundHandler> => {
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: NotFoundResponse must be exported from the package entrypoint so users can reference it when augmenting the module.
forces.quote:
+  NotFoundResponse,
 } from './types'
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users can declare a module augmentation that extends NotFoundResponse with a TypedResponse to produce typed 404 responses consumable by hc.
