decisions.title: Add buildSearchParams option to RPC client for customizable query serialization
decisions.summary: The default client behavior serializes array query parameters as repeated keys (e.g., key=a&key=b), which does not suit all server-side conventions. A new buildSearchParams option is added to ClientRequestOptions, allowing users to provide a custom function that serializes query parameters into URLSearchParams.

context.claim: The default client behavior serializes array query parameters as repeated keys (e.g., key=a&key=b), which does not suit all server-side conventions.
context.quote: This allows users to customize how query parameters are serialized, for example using bracket notation (key[]=value) for arrays instead of the default behavior (key=value1&key=value2).
context.author: bolasblack

context.claim: The author had a concrete use case requiring this customization.
context.quote: This feature would be very helpful for my use case and doesn't appear to impact any existing code, so it might be a good candidate for the next release.
context.author: bolasblack

decision.claim: A new buildSearchParams option is added to ClientRequestOptions, allowing users to provide a custom function that serializes query parameters into URLSearchParams.
decision.quote: Custom function to serialize query parameters into URLSearchParams.
decision.author: bolasblack

decision.claim: The maintainer approved and merged the change into the next branch for the next minor version.
decision.quote: Looks good! I'll merge this to the next branch for the next minor version and release it soon.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The default serialization uses repeated keys for arrays, which is incompatible with servers expecting bracket notation.
forces.quote: By default, arrays are serialized as multiple parameters with the same key (e.g., `key=a&key=b`).
forces.author: bolasblack

forces.claim: The change should not impact existing code, keeping it backward-compatible.
forces.quote: doesn't appear to impact any existing code
forces.author: bolasblack

consequences.claim: Users can now integrate libraries like qs for bracket-notation serialization via a user-supplied function.
consequences.quote: You can provide a custom function to change this behavior, for example to use bracket notation (e.g., `key[]=a&key[]=b`).
consequences.author: bolasblack

consequences.claim: The feature is targeted for the v4.11 minor release on the next branch.
consequences.quote: I'll merge this to the next branch for the next minor version and release it soon.
consequences.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Replace schema-based path tracking with CurrentPath type parameter for pathless handlers
decisions.summary: The PR references issue #4529 as the prior work / context for this change. The team decided to adopt the CurrentPath type-parameter approach for tracking the path used by pathless handlers.

context.claim: At runtime, pathless handlers use `this.#path` (the last registered path) for routing, so any type-level path tracking must match that behavior.
context.quote: in the actual routing, `this.#path` (which stores the last registered path) is used for the pathless handler
context.author: kosei28

context.claim: The underlying problem the fix addresses is that the type system did not know the 'current path' used at runtime for pathless handlers.
context.quote: The problem was that types did not know the "current path".
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The PR references issue #4529 as the prior work / context for this change.
context.author: kosei28

decision.claim: The PR was approved and merged into the `next` branch for the upcoming minor release.
decision.quote: I'll merge this to the `next` branch for the next minor version and release it soon.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: kosei28 reverted to the CurrentPath approach and fixed the related type updates for pathless app.use() and multi-path app.on().
decision.quote: Reverted to `CurrentPath` approach and fixed type updates for pathless `app.use()` and multi-path `app.on()`.
decision.author: kosei28

decision.claim: The team decided to adopt the CurrentPath type-parameter approach for tracking the path used by pathless handlers.
decision.quote: Ah, you are right! The `CurrentPath` approach is best.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Type tracking for pathless handlers must align with actual runtime routing behavior, which uses the last registered path.
forces.quote: in the actual routing, `this.#path` (which stores the last registered path) is used for the pathless handler
forces.author: kosei28

forces.claim: Author prefers the CurrentPath approach because it is simpler to implement and aligns better with actual behavior.
forces.quote: I think the `CurrentPath` approach is simpler to implement and aligns better with the actual behavior.
forces.author: kosei28

forces.claim: Type-branching based on the handler's return type was deemed unnecessary because calling next() routes to subsequent handlers regardless of return type.
forces.quote: even if the handler's return value isn't `Promise<void>`, calling `next` will route to the subsequent handler, so I don't think type branching based on the return type is necessary.
forces.author: kosei28

forces.claim: Type-instantiation performance is a concern; the CurrentPath approach was measured at 684,676 instantiations.
forces.quote: The number of instantiations for the `CurrentPath` approach is 684,676.
forces.author: kosei28

consequences.claim: Type-instantiation count is 684,676 for the chosen CurrentPath approach (down from 694,649 for an earlier variant).
consequences.quote: The number of instantiations for the `CurrentPath` approach is 684,676.
consequences.author: kosei28

consequences.claim: The generated schema for a pathless middleware applied with `.use('*', middleware)` changes from `{ '*': {} }` to `{}`, which is acceptable for real-world use cases.
consequences.quote: Though you change the test, I also think it's okay. It's not a problem for real-world use cases.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: CI failures occur due to const type parameters conflicting with the old esbuild version; a separate PR (#4571) is required to fix the build.
consequences.quote: Tests pass locally, but CI is failing due to const type parameters conflicting with the old esbuild version. #4571 will fix the build.
consequences.author: kosei28

alternatives.name: VoidMiddlewarePath approach (track pathless-middleware paths via a dedicated type parameter based on Promise<void> return)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Initially favored by the maintainer, but abandoned after the author pointed out that the CurrentPath approach is simpler and aligns better with actual runtime behavior; the maintainer agreed that the real problem was types not knowing the current path.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ah, you are right! The `CurrentPath` approach is best. The problem was that types did not know the "current path". Let's go with it.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: ExtractStringKey approach (schema-based path tracking, from #4529)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Produced a schema shape that diverged from actual routing behavior; superseded by the CurrentPath approach, which was chosen as the fix for #4529.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: `ExtractStringKey` approach (from #4529)
alternatives.author: kosei28

alternatives.name: Make pathless handler's path a union of all registered paths to match the original type
alternatives.rejection_reason: Does not match actual runtime routing, which only uses the last registered path (`this.#path`) for pathless handlers; the author judged the earlier CurrentPath implementation 'might have been better'.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I fixed the pathless handler's path to be a union to match the original type. However, in the actual routing, `this.#path` (which stores the last registered path) is used for the pathless handler, resulting in the following behavior. The `CurrentPath` implementation before the fix might have been better.
alternatives.author: kosei28

decisions.title: Preserve literal union and optional types in validator input type inference via new InferInput helper
decisions.summary: The query input type was incorrectly inferred when it contained a union or an optional field, widening literal unions like 'asc' | 'desc' to string | string[]. Introduce a new InferInput type in utils.ts to correctly infer validator input types, preserving literal unions and optional fields.

context.claim: The query input type was incorrectly inferred when it contained a union or an optional field, widening literal unions like 'asc' | 'desc' to string | string[].
context.quote: This PR will fix the issue where the query input type is incorrectly typed when it has a union or an optional.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: Concretely, a literal union field like orderBy: 'asc' | 'desc' was being inferred on the client as string | string[] instead of the original literal union.
context.quote: orderBy: 'asc', // ❌️ string | string[] => should be ⭕️ 'asc' | 'desc'
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: Optional literal union fields were also being widened to string | string[] | undefined instead of preserving the literal union.
context.quote: ordreByWithdefault: 'asc', // ❌️ string | string[] | undefined => should be ⭕️ 'asc' | 'desc' | undefined
context.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Introduce a new InferInput type in utils.ts to correctly infer validator input types, preserving literal unions and optional fields.
decision.quote: To make it easier, I created an `InferInput` type in `utils.ts`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: InferInput is exported so downstream validators can adopt it without bumping peerDependencies on hono.
decision.quote: export InferInput
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Optional field support was added as part of this fix.
decision.quote: support optional
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The fix had to be usable by third-party validators like @hono/zod-validator without forcing a hono peerDependency bump.
forces.quote: If you want to fix `@hono/zod-validator` or another module, you can copy the `InferInput` code from the file. If so, you don't have to update the `hono` version in `peerDependencies`.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Runtime bundle size is unchanged at 18,377 bytes; this is a pure type-level change.
consequences.author: github-actions

consequences.claim: Literal union query values (e.g. 'asc' | 'desc') are now preserved in client type inference instead of being widened to string | string[].
consequences.quote: orderBy: 'asc', // ❌️ string | string[] => should be ⭕️ 'asc' | 'desc'
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Optional literal union fields now preserve their literal union with undefined rather than being widened to string | string[] | undefined.
consequences.quote: ordreByWithdefault: 'asc', // ❌️ string | string[] | undefined => should be ⭕️ 'asc' | 'desc' | undefined
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Third-party validators can copy the InferInput code into their own package to get the fix without upgrading hono's peerDependency.
consequences.quote: you can copy the `InferInput` code from the file. If so, you don't have to update the `hono` version in `peerDependencies`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Add redirect plugin to hono/ssg as a built-in plugin
decisions.summary: Redirects are needed for SSG to properly direct users and search engines when URLs change, supporting migrations and preserving SEO. Add a redirectPlugin under hono/ssg that generates HTML redirect pages for 3xx responses with a Location header.

context.claim: Before the plugin, toSSG() produced empty text files for endpoints that performed a redirect, which was considered undesirable.
context.quote: Currently, `toSSG()` outputs an empty text file for endpoints that perform a redirect. However, I don't think this behavior is desirable for most users.
context.author: 3w36zj6

context.claim: Redirects are needed for SSG to properly direct users and search engines when URLs change, supporting migrations and preserving SEO.
context.quote: Redirects are essential for SSG to ensure users and search engines are properly directed when URLs change. This supports smooth migrations, maintains SEO value, and prevents broken links.
context.author: 3w36zj6

decision.claim: Add a redirectPlugin under hono/ssg that generates HTML redirect pages for 3xx responses with a Location header.
decision.quote: feat(ssg): add redirect plugin
decision.author: 3w36zj6

decision.claim: The plugin will be managed under hono/ssg rather than as a separate third-party package.
decision.quote: Then, let's go with managing it under `hono/ssg`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Redirect handling is provided as an opt-in plugin rather than part of the default plugin, because whether to redirect with HTML is a user decision.
decision.quote: Redirecting with HTML is something the user should decide. So it's better not to include it in the default plugin.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The plugin triggers on 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 responses for spec consistency.
decision.quote: fix(ssg): support 303, 307, 308 as redirect triggers
decision.author: 3w36zj6

decision.claim: The redirectPlugin is exposed as a function rather than a plain object.
decision.quote: Ahh, I think `function` is better because the user can pass options like `redirectPlugin({ optionA })` if needed.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Approval and merge into v4.12 release.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The HTTP spec requires redirect responses to include a Location header; the plugin skips generation when absent.
forces.quote: The specification mandates that redirect responses must include a `Location` header [^1]. Current implementations simply bypass generating the file if no `Location` header is present
forces.author: 3w36zj6

forces.claim: All 3xx redirect statuses (301, 302, 303, 307, 308) require Location headers, motivating consistent handling across them.
forces.quote: should we also treat 303[^1], 307[^2], and 308[^3] as triggers for generating redirect HTML? Since all of them require the `Location` header, I feel it would make the implementation more consistent with the specification.
forces.author: 3w36zj6

forces.claim: Built-in plugins should be lightweight, so multiple plugins are consolidated in a single plugins.ts file.
forces.quote: Since built-in plugins should only handle lightweight ones, I decided to manage multiple plugins in a single `plugins.ts` file.
forces.author: 3w36zj6

forces.claim: Plugin ordering matters: redirectPlugin must be placed above defaultPlugin to work correctly when combined.
forces.quote: It works well if you put the `redirecPlugin` above the `defaultPlugin`. So, I think it's good to add a description about it on our website.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Output must escape the redirect target to avoid injection if a script is passed into `to`.
forces.quote: It's a rare case if passing the script into `to`, but I think you should escape it. How about `html`?
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Using Hono's existing routing (e.g., `app.get('/old', c => c.redirect('/new'))`) is preferred over an Astro-like redirects map because it handles complex rules like parameterized paths.
forces.quote:
Imagine if the user wants to write a complex rule like

```ts
app.get('/old/:path', (c) => c.redirect(`/new/${c.req.param('path')}`))
```

How does the user write the rule? Using regexp? It's one idea, but using Hono's basic routing is better; it's already implemented.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The `from` parameter was dropped from the redirect HTML because the user already knows the source URL they accessed.
consequences.quote:
How about removing `from` from this PR?

At the very least, the user knows where it comes from because they access the URL directly. And I think the information about `from` is not so important.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The feature is experimental; its API may change.
consequences.quote:
`redirectPlugin` is an experimental feature.
 * The API might be changed.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Plugin dependency validation (name/requires fields) is deferred to a separate discussion.
consequences.quote:
> I think dependency validation itself is an interesting idea, but we need to carefully discuss the interface and validation methods.

Yes. Let's discuss another place.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The plugin may later be moved to Third-party Middleware depending on how it evolves.
consequences.quote: Since it's still an experimental feature, we can consider moving it to Third-party Middleware in the future, depending on how things go.
consequences.author: 3w36zj6

consequences.claim: Shipped in the v4.12.0 minor release as a feature.
consequences.quote: This change is a "feature", so we will include it in the next minor release v4.12.0.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Built-in plugin tests were separated into their own file as ssg.test.tsx grew too large.
consequences.quote: The `ssg.test.tsx` became fat. So separating it is a good idea.
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Astro-style `redirects` key-value mapping option on ToSSGOptions
alternatives.rejection_reason: Using Hono's existing routing (c.redirect) already handles the use case and supports complex parameterized rules that a flat key-value map cannot.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
I think using `app.get("/old", c => c.redirect("/new"))` is okay and we don't need `redirect` option.

`redirect` needs "routing" for the old path to the new path.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Include redirect HTML generation directly in the defaultPlugin
alternatives.rejection_reason: Whether to emit HTML redirect pages is a user decision, not a default behavior.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Redirecting with HTML is something the user should decide. So it's better not to include it in the default plugin.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Provide the redirect plugin in a separate @hono/ssg-plugins-essential package
alternatives.rejection_reason: The plugin feels close to core SSG functionality, so managing it under hono/ssg was chosen for now; it can be moved later if needed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think it might be a good idea to manage it under `hono/ssg` for now, given that the plugin feels closer to the core functionality.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Track redirect source paths via a beforeRequestHook FIFO queue to populate the `from` field
alternatives.rejection_reason: The approach could break under parallel execution and would require a proper FIFO queue; ultimately the `from` field was removed entirely since users already know the source URL.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I also thought of another option, but this approach might break when running in parallel. (For production use, we would need to use a FIFO queue, but in this PoC I implemented it in a straightforward way.)
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Use a custom header like `x-hono-ssg-route` to inject the original request path into the response
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered as a way to recover the `from` path, but abandoned when the `from` parameter itself was removed from the redirect HTML.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: The `Response` object in `afterResponseHook` does not contain information about the original redirect source, so we probably need to prepare a custom header like `x-hono-ssg-route` to inject the request path.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Expose the redirect plugin as a plain object (matching defaultPlugin's original shape)
alternatives.rejection_reason: A function form was preferred so users can pass options like `redirectPlugin({ optionA })`; defaultPlugin was also converted to a function for consistency.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Ahh, I think `function` is better because the user can pass options like `redirectPlugin({ optionA })` if needed.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Use `export *` from ./plugins in index.ts
alternatives.rejection_reason: Explicit named exports were preferred over a wildcard re-export.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think the current explicit implementation is okay.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Only trigger on 301 and 302 redirects
alternatives.rejection_reason: Excluding 303/307/308 was inconsistent with the HTTP spec since all require Location headers; coverage was expanded to all five statuses.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: should we also treat 303[^1], 307[^2], and 308[^3] as triggers for generating redirect HTML? Since all of them require the `Location` header, I feel it would make the implementation more consistent with the specification.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

alternatives.name: Warn or throw an error when a redirect response lacks a Location header
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered but the plugin simply bypasses file generation in that case.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Current implementations simply bypass generating the file if no `Location` header is present, though options include displaying a warning or throwing an error could be considered.
alternatives.author: 3w36zj6

decisions.title: Expand $all to standard HTTP methods in RPC ClientRequest type
decisions.summary: The RPC client incorrectly exposed $all as a valid method when routes were defined with app.all() Expand $all into all standard HTTP methods ($get, $post, $put, $delete, $options, $patch) on the ClientRequest type, rather than just excluding $all

context.claim: TypeScript was not erroring on invalid $all usage even though there is no ALL HTTP method
context.quote: TypeScript should throw the error because there is no method: `ALL`, so `$all` is invalid.
context.author: nakasyou

context.claim: A route defined with app.all can accept any method at runtime, so simply excluding $all from types leaves the client unable to type-check valid calls like $get
context.quote: If you define `app.all`, you can send any method from the client. So should `$get()` typed? In the following code, `client['all-route'].$get()` is not typed. But the client can send GET request and does not throw runtime errors.
context.author: yusukebe

context.claim: The RPC client incorrectly exposed $all as a valid method when routes were defined with app.all()
context.quote: Fixes the RPC client incorrectly exposing `$all` as a valid method when using `app.all()`
context.author: paveg

context.claim: HTTP has no ALL method, so $all should not be exposed on the client
context.quote: Since HTTP has no "ALL" method, the client should not expose `$all`
context.author: paveg

decision.claim: Expand $all into all standard HTTP methods ($get, $post, $put, $delete, $options, $patch) on the ClientRequest type, rather than just excluding $all
decision.quote: Now $all is expanded to all standard HTTP methods ($get, $post, $put, $delete, $options, $patch), matching the actual runtime behavior.
decision.author: paveg

decision.claim: Implementation uses METHODS and METHOD_NAME_ALL_LOWERCASE constants from router.ts rather than hardcoded strings
decision.quote: import { METHODS, METHOD_NAME_ALL_LOWERCASE } from '../router'
decision.author: paveg

decision.claim: The maintainer explicitly endorsed the expansion approach proposed by the author
decision.quote: Yeees! That approch is great. I love it.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Method name references should use constants from router.ts rather than hardcoded 'all'
forces.quote: I think we have to use the constant instead of `all` hard-coded.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Only standard HTTP methods should be exposed; custom/nonstandard HTTP methods are not popular enough to justify a generic type
forces.quote: Using custom HTTP methods rather than official HTTP methods, GET, POST, and others, is not popular. Therefore, this way is not bad.
forces.author: nakasyou

forces.claim: Client types must match runtime behavior: app.all() accepts any HTTP method at runtime, so the client should type-check those calls
forces.quote: But the client can send GET request and does not throw runtime errors.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: client['all-route'].$get() and other standard HTTP method calls on app.all routes are now properly typed, matching runtime behavior
consequences.quote: This would make `client['all-route'].$get()`  properly typed, matching the runtime behavior.
consequences.author: paveg

consequences.claim: A small performance decrease is accepted as a tradeoff for correct $all behavior
consequences.quote: The performance decrease is unwelcome but slight. It’s acceptable. Correcting the behavior of `$all` is worth more than that.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: No recursive type computation is introduced; overhead consists of one conditional type, a mapped type over 6 keys, and Omit
consequences.quote: No recursive type computation is introduced. The slight memory increase (~1.2%) is negligible and within acceptable bounds.
consequences.author: paveg

consequences.claim: TypeScript memory usage during type-checking increases by ~1.2% (from 704,748K to 713,348K)
consequences.author: paveg

alternatives.name: Hardcode the string 'all' in the Exclude type
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer requested using the existing METHOD_NAME_ALL_LOWERCASE constant from router.ts instead of a hardcoded string for consistency.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think we have to use the constant instead of `all` hard-coded.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Define a generic type covering any custom HTTP methods (not just standard ones)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Using custom, non-standard HTTP methods is not popular, so restricting to the standard HTTP method set ($get, $post, $put, $delete, $options, $patch) is acceptable.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Using custom HTTP methods rather than official HTTP methods, GET, POST, and others, is not popular. Therefore, this way is not bad.
alternatives.author: nakasyou

alternatives.name: Simply exclude $all from ClientRequest via Exclude<M, '$all'>
alternatives.rejection_reason: Excluding $all removes the invalid key but does not expose the standard HTTP methods ($get, $post, etc.) that app.all() actually supports at runtime, leaving a runtime/type mismatch where valid calls like client['all-route'].$get() are untyped.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: simply excluding `$all` doesn't expose standard HTTP methods (`$get`, `$post`, etc.) for routes defined with `app.all()`.
alternatives.author: paveg

decisions.title: Export getBunServer from hono/bun and make it generic over server type
decisions.summary: Accessing the Bun server instance in handlers was previously possible only via c.env, which was not straightforward. getBunServer is exported from hono/bun as a public API.

context.claim: Accessing the Bun server instance in handlers was previously possible only via c.env, which was not straightforward.
context.quote: Exported `getBunServer` from `hono/bun` to be able to access Bun server instance in handlers. It was possible before via `c.env`, but this change makes it straightforward.

decision.claim: getBunServer is exported from hono/bun as a public API.
decision.quote: Exported `getBunServer` from `hono/bun` to be able to access Bun server instance in handlers.
decision.author: artemtam

decision.claim: getBunServer is made generic, requiring users to pass a server type.
decision.quote: Also, made `getBunServer` a generic that requires a server type, so users can set it correctly.
decision.author: artemtam

decision.claim: The maintainer approved the change and scheduled it for a patch release.
decision.quote: Looks good! It's a new feature, but a slight change. So I'm planning to add it in a patch version.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Internal call sites (getConnInfo.ts and websocket.ts) need to specify the specific server fields they use, motivating a generic signature.
forces.quote: It makes more clear internally as well - `getConnInfo.ts` and `websocket.ts` specify fields they need.
forces.author: artemtam

alternatives.name: Access the Bun server via c.env (status quo)
alternatives.rejection_reason: It was already possible but not straightforward; exporting a dedicated helper makes the access path explicit.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: It was possible before via `c.env`, but this change makes it straightforward.
alternatives.author: artemtam

decisions.title: Add `alwaysRedirect` option to trailing-slash middleware to support wildcard routes
decisions.summary: The `trimTrailingSlash` middleware does not trim trailing slashes for routes that use the wildcard `*`. Add an `alwaysRedirect` option to `trimTrailingSlash` and `appendTrailingSlash` that redirects before handlers execute, fixing wildcard route handling.

context.claim: The `trimTrailingSlash` middleware does not trim trailing slashes for routes that use the wildcard `*`.
context.quote: `trimTrailingSlash` doesn't work on paths with `*`
context.author: victormihalache

context.claim: The root cause is that the middleware only redirects when the route is not found (404), so wildcard matches skip the redirect.
context.quote: To fix the issue all that needs to be done is remove the check for a 404 page
context.author: victormihalache

context.claim: Users expect the middleware to trim the trailing slash on any matched page, not only unhandled ones.
context.quote: When I use this middleware I actually _expect_ any page, regardless of anything else, to get the trailing slash trimmed; therefore I don't understand why if the page is found one should not obey that rule.
context.author: victormihalache

context.claim: Handlers were being executed even though the request would ultimately be redirected, which is wasteful.
context.quote: we should do the redirecting before `await next()`, because we will also be running the handler/s even tho we end upon redirecting the request
context.author: MathurAditya724

decision.claim: Add an `alwaysRedirect` option to `trimTrailingSlash` and `appendTrailingSlash` that redirects before handlers execute, fixing wildcard route handling.

decision.claim: When `alwaysRedirect: true`, the middleware redirects before executing handlers, which fixes wildcard routes.

decision.claim: The reviewer accepted `alwaysRedirect` as the final name.

forces.claim: The option name had to be unambiguous — `strict` could be interpreted in either direction (always redirect vs. only redirect on 404).

forces.claim: The author agreed `strict` was confusing because it could be interpreted either way.

consequences.quote: This obviously executes before the handler with `eager:true`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The default behavior remains unchanged (only redirect on 404), so existing users are unaffected.
consequences.quote: If `false` (default), it will only redirect when the route is not found (404).
consequences.author: codler

alternatives.name: Unconditionally remove the 404 check (make always-redirect the default, no opt-in)

alternatives.name: Split behavior across two separate middleware routes (use `trimTrailingSlash` and `appendTrailingSlash` on different sub-routers instead of an option)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Suggested as a workaround in the linked issue for users needing exceptions, but the maintainers ultimately resolved the core wildcard bug by adding the `alwaysRedirect` option rather than requiring users to split routers.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think the best way for you to configure this would be to have 2 different route handlers, One with the `trimTrailingSlash` middleware and the other with `appendTrailingSlash` to enforce it on them.
alternatives.author: MathurAditya724

alternatives.name: Name the option `strict`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Ambiguous — `strict` can be read as either 'more limited, only applied to 404' or as 'always redirect', so it could be interpreted in either sense.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I thought `strict` was confusing, since I saw your comment that we can interpret it in either sense.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Invert the logic and keep `strict` with inverted default (strict=true means only 404)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Reviewer proposed inverting the semantics so `strict` would mean 'only applied to 404' as the default, but the author instead proposed renaming to avoid the ambiguity entirely.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Should we invert the logic? When I hear an option strict it is more limited, hence only applied to 404.
alternatives.author: codler

alternatives.name: Name the option `eager`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Proposed by the author as a replacement for `strict`, but the reviewer found `alwaysRedirect` better and it was replaced.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: It is better. How about `alwaysRedirect?: boolean`?
alternatives.author: codler

decisions.title: Add fast path to c.json() matching c.text() optimization to skip #newResponse() and Headers allocation
decisions.summary: c.text() already has a fast path that returns new Response(text) directly when no status, headers, or finalized state exists Add the same 5-condition fast path to c.json() as exists in c.text()

context.claim: c.text() already has a fast path that returns new Response(text) directly when no status, headers, or finalized state exists
context.quote: `c.text()` already has a fast path (added in an earlier optimization) that returns `new Response(text)` directly when no status, headers, or finalized state exists.
context.author: mgcrea

context.claim: In @hono/node-server, a plain inline header object skips the buildOutgoingHttpHeaders() conversion that a Headers instance requires
context.quote: When the adapter writes the response, it checks `header instanceof Headers` — a plain object skips the `buildOutgoingHttpHeaders()` conversion entirely, while a `Headers` instance requires iteration and conversion to `OutgoingHttpHeaders`.
context.author: mgcrea

context.claim: c.json() was missing the equivalent optimization and always went through #newResponse(), which allocates a Headers object and iterates header entries even for simple c.json() calls
context.quote: `c.json()` was missing this optimization and always went through `#newResponse()`, which unconditionally allocates a `Headers` object and iterates header entries — even for the simplest `c.json({ ... })` call.
context.author: mgcrea

decision.claim: The maintainer approved the PR
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: A refactor was applied to extract the fast-path predicate into a shared helper
decision.quote: refactor with `useFastPath`
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The maintainer ultimately chose to use Response.json() for the fast path and refactored to a shared useFastPath helper
decision.quote: use `Response.json()`
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: When all five conditions are met, create the Response directly with an inline header object instead of going through #newResponse()
decision.quote: When all conditions are met (the common case for simple JSON responses), it creates the Response directly with an inline header object instead of going through `#newResponse()`
decision.author: mgcrea

decision.claim: Add the same 5-condition fast path to c.json() as exists in c.text()
decision.quote: This PR adds the same 5-condition fast path to `c.json()`.
decision.author: mgcrea

forces.claim: The optimization is aimed at the common case of simple JSON responses
forces.quote: When all conditions are met (the common case for simple JSON responses)
forces.author: mgcrea

forces.claim: The fast path must only apply when no prepared headers, no status, no arg, no headers, and not finalized — these are the five conditions that guarantee behavioral equivalence with the slow path
forces.quote: !this.#preparedHeaders && !this.#status && !arg && !headers && !this.finalized
forces.author: mgcrea

forces.claim: The change has a compounding effect with @hono/node-server's inline-header-object detection path
forces.quote: In the `@hono/node-server` adapter, this has a compounding effect. The lightweight `Response` constructor stores inline header objects directly in its cache.
forces.author: mgcrea

consequences.claim: File size increased slightly as a result of the added fast-path code
consequences.quote: Sorry! I completely misunderstood. The file size is slightly increased.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Query benchmark improved by 14.9% and Body by 76.7%, with an average improvement of 24.8%
consequences.author: mgcrea

consequences.claim: Extracting the fast-path predicate into a shared function reduces file size with negligible overhead
consequences.quote: Using a common function reduces the file size slightly. I think the overhead can be ignored.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Ping benchmark improved by 8.2% (55,183 → 59,697)
consequences.author: mgcrea

alternatives.name: Inline the 5-condition fast-path predicate in each of c.text() and c.json()
alternatives.rejection_reason: Maintainer proposed extracting the predicate into a shared #shouldFastPath / useFastPath helper to reduce file size with negligible overhead; the final commit adopted this refactor.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Using a common function reduces the file size slightly. I think the overhead can be ignored.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Keep c.json() routed through #newResponse() (status quo, no fast path)

decisions.title: Add exports field to ExecutionContext as `any`, relying on `declare module` for Cloudflare Workers typing
decisions.summary: Cloudflare Workers exposes a new `exports` property on ExecutionContext (behind `enable_ctx_exports`) that Hono's ExecutionContext does not surface. The final decision was to type `exports` as `any` on the built-in ExecutionContext interface and rely on TypeScript `declare module` augmentation for typed autocompletion.

context.claim: Cloudflare Workers exposes a new `exports` property on ExecutionContext (behind `enable_ctx_exports`) that Hono's ExecutionContext does not surface.
context.quote: Creating a Cloudflare Workers app with `enable_ctx_exports` compatibility flag does not gives access to new property `exports` that should list all top-level exports like DurableObjects.
context.author: unknown

context.claim: The PR's stated goal is to add the `exports` property to ExecutionContext for Cloudflare Workers.
context.quote: Add `exports` property to `ExecutionContext` for Cloudflare Workers
context.author: toreis-up

context.claim: The linked issue proposed making ExecutionContext overridable via an Env generic slot similar to Bindings and Variables.
context.quote:
It could be achieved for example by passing ExecutionContext the same way as Binding and Variables:

```ts
const routes = new Hono<{ Bindings: CloudflareBindings; Variables: HonoAuthorizationContext, ExecutionContext: ExecutionContext }>();
```
context.author: unknown

decision.claim: The final decision was to type `exports` as `any` on the built-in ExecutionContext interface and rely on TypeScript `declare module` augmentation for typed autocompletion.
decision.quote: So, I think we should make it `any` and encourage the use of `declare module`.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The author agreed to this approach, confirming consensus.
decision.quote: That would be nice. I agree with it.
decision.author: toreis-up

decision.claim: The maintainer confirmed acceptance of the `any` approach on merge.
decision.quote: In the end, we use `any`, but it's good. Thank you for your contribution.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Users opt into typed `exports` by augmenting the ExecutionContext interface via `declare module 'hono'`.
decision.author: toreis-up

forces.claim: ExecutionContext is essentially a Cloudflare-Workers-specific concept, so the maintainer wants to avoid making the core type system platform-specific.
forces.quote: Basically, ExecutionContext is used only for Cloudflare Workers. We don’t have to make it platform-specific in the core.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The maintainer wants to avoid adding complexity to type definitions during the current major version.
forces.quote: the big problem is that it increases the type definition. Honestly, I don't want to make it more complex at the current major version.
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: IntelliSense/autocomplete with Wrangler-generated types must continue to work.
forces.quote: As you said, not having IntelliSense working is not good.
forces.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: Users who want typed `exports` must write a `declare module 'hono'` augmentation referencing `Cloudflare.Exports`.
consequences.author: yusukebe

consequences.claim: The change will be shipped as part of v4.12.0.
consequences.quote: I'll include this change in the next minor version, v4.12.0. Will be released soon!
consequences.author: yusukebe

alternatives.name: Make `exports` optional (`exports?: unknown`) instead of a required field on ExecutionContext
alternatives.rejection_reason: Raised by the Copilot reviewer to avoid a breaking change for consumers and to avoid forcing adapters/tests to pass `exports: {}`; the maintainers instead went with `any` (which sidesteps the required-ness concern by accepting anything), rather than `unknown`/optional.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Since `ExecutionContext` is a public exported type, making a new required field is a breaking change for consumers implementing/typing against this interface (and forces adapters/tests to pass `exports: {}` even when the runtime has no such field). Consider making `exports` optional (and possibly `unknown`)
alternatives.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

alternatives.name: Fall back `ExecutionContextOf<E>` to the built-in ExecutionContext when `E['ExecutionContext']` is `unknown`/`any` (using IsAny/NonNullable logic)
alternatives.rejection_reason: This refinement was proposed to fix a regression where `new Hono<Env>()` loses default ExecutionContext typing; it became moot once the entire generic-slot approach was abandoned in favor of typing `exports` as `any` plus `declare module`.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: `ExecutionContextOf<Env>` currently resolves to `unknown` because `Env['ExecutionContext']` is `unknown`. That means users who write `new Hono<Env>()` (using the exported `Env` alias) lose the default `ExecutionContext` typing.
alternatives.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

alternatives.name: Cast at the call site, e.g. `(c.executionCtx as globalThis.ExecutionContext).exports`
alternatives.rejection_reason: Proposed by the author as a workaround if `exports` were typed as `any`; not adopted because `declare module` augmentation was shown to provide proper typing without casting.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted:
I think this can be resolved by casting like following:
`(c.executionCtx as globalThis.ExecutionContext).exports;`
alternatives.author: toreis-up

alternatives.name: Add `ExecutionContext` as a generic slot on `Env` (alongside Bindings and Variables), with an `ExecutionContextOf<E>` helper
alternatives.rejection_reason: The maintainer considered passing a generic to define the ExecutionContext type overkill, since ExecutionContext is Cloudflare-specific and shouldn't be made platform-specific in the core; it also increases the type definition complexity during the current major version.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I think passing a generic to define the ExecutionContext type is overkill. Basically, ExecutionContext is used only for Cloudflare Workers. We don’t have to make it platform-specific in the core.
alternatives.author: yusukebe

decisions.title: Optimize TrieRouter hot path: remove spread, O(1) hasChildren, lazy regex
decisions.summary: The TrieRouter had performance bottlenecks from spread syntax expansion, O(N) hasChildren checks, eager regex generation, and redundant processing. Apply the set of TrieRouter micro-optimizations and ship them in the next minor release v4.12.0.

context.claim: The TrieRouter had performance bottlenecks from spread syntax expansion, O(N) hasChildren checks, eager regex generation, and redundant processing.
context.quote:
1. Removed spread syntax to improve expansion time.
2. Reduced time complexity of `hasChildren` from $O(N)$ to $O(1)$ to resolve a performance bottleneck.
3. Implemented lazy evaluation to prevent unnecessary regular expression generation.
4. Removed redundant processes to streamline execution.

decisions.title: Replace regex-based Accept header parsing with single-pass character scanner to mitigate ReDoS
decisions.summary: The existing regex-based split in the Accept header parser is vulnerable to ReDoS when given crafted headers with many segments. The Accept header parser is rewritten as a single-pass character scanner instead of regex-based splitting.

context.claim: The existing regex-based split in the Accept header parser is vulnerable to ReDoS when given crafted headers with many segments.
context.quote: In proxy configurations without header size limits, this can cause real impact.
context.author: EdamAme-x

context.claim: Parsing time scales super-linearly with header segment count (12.8ms at 4000 segments, 51.07ms at 8000, 203.52ms at 16000).
context.author: EdamAme-x

context.claim: A maintainer confirmed the existing regex has ReDoS potential.
context.quote: It's definitely a regular expression with potential ReDoS issues.
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: The existing parser has correctness bugs beyond ReDoS — it fails to handle commas inside double-quoted parameter values, even though such headers are valid.
context.quote:
With the current code, it can't handle patterns where there's a comma inside double quotes either (even though this is a valid header)...
```
type;a=1;b="2,2"
```
context.author: usualoma

context.claim: Existing tests encoded incorrect expected values, masking parser bugs.
context.quote: I believe the following current test is buggy because the expected value is incorrect. (b should be `2`, not `"2"`, and e is invalid, so it should either throw an error or be ignored).
context.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The Accept header parser is rewritten as a single-pass character scanner instead of regex-based splitting.
decision.quote:
I'd like to take this opportunity to handle the entire parsing in one pass.

(It will require a complete rewrite, but) I've created a PR against your branch.
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The single-pass rewrite was pulled into this PR via merge of usualoma's branch.
decision.quote: Sorry! I missed it. I've just merged it now!

decision.claim: The merge commit brings in the single-pass refactor from the accept-single-pass branch.
decision.quote:
Merge pull request #12 from usualoma/accept-single-pass

refactor: improve accept header parsing

decision.claim: The maintainer approved with LGTM and merged.
decision.quote: @EdamAme-x @usualoma Thank you! Merging.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: The fix must eliminate the ReDoS attack surface in Accept header parsing.
forces.quote: I believe this PR will resolve the ReDoS issue

forces.claim: The parser must correctly handle quoted parameter values containing commas, which the previous implementation could not.
forces.quote: With the current code, it can't handle patterns where there's a comma inside double quotes either (even though this is a valid header)...
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The solution should not regress performance on standard header inputs.
forces.quote: I believe this PR will resolve the ReDoS issue and improve performance.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: The single-pass parser is measurably faster than both the original regex approach and the intermediate splitByDelimiter approach across all benchmarked inputs (1.22x–2.87x faster than regex, 2.96x–4.94x faster than splitByDelimiter).
consequences.quote:
main  c829099 (single-pass) - benchmarks/accept/accept.bench.ts > standard: 3 types with q
    2.87x faster than main (regex)
    3.86x faster than 947b22f4 (splitByDelimiter)

decisions.title: Align JSX link hoisting and dedupe with React 19 (stylesheet+precedence only)
decisions.summary: A <link rel="canonical"> tag was silently dropped from rendered HTML when a <link rel="alternate" hreflang="..."> tag shared the same href. Align Hono JSX runtime link hoisting/dedupe behavior with React 19's model, which dedupes only stylesheet links with precedence.

context.claim: A <link rel="canonical"> tag was silently dropped from rendered HTML when a <link rel="alternate" hreflang="..."> tag shared the same href.
context.quote: When using `jsxRenderer`, a `<link rel="canonical">` tag is silently dropped from the rendered HTML if a `<link rel="alternate" hreflang="...">` tag shares the same `href`.
context.author: shinagawa-web

context.claim: The root cause was that link dedupe used href as key with OR semantics across keys.
context.quote: The deduplication logic in `src/jsx/intrinsic-element/components.ts` uses **OR semantics**: if *any* key in `deDupeKeys` matches an existing tag's props, the new tag is considered a duplicate.
context.author: shinagawa-web

context.claim: The bug was confirmed present on the latest main branch (v4.12.5).
context.quote: Confirmed that the latest `main` branch (v4.12.5) still has the same behavior — `link: ['href']` in `common.ts` and the OR-semantics deduplication in `components.ts` are unchanged.
context.author: shinagawa-web

decision.claim: Over-dedup by href for canonical/alternate/preload/icon/preconnect is fixed, and dedupe is narrowed to stylesheet links with precedence only.
decision.quote: It fixes over-deduplication by href (for canonical / alternate / preload / icon / preconnect, etc.) and narrows dedupe to stylesheet links with precedence only.
decision.author: usualoma

decision.claim: The PR was approved by the maintainer.
decision.quote: LGTM!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Align Hono JSX runtime link hoisting/dedupe behavior with React 19's model, which dedupes only stylesheet links with precedence.
decision.quote: Because React 19 appears to dedupe only link elements that are stylesheet + precedence, this PR aligns Hono JSX runtime link hoisting/dedupe behavior with that model.
decision.author: usualoma

forces.claim: React 19 only dedupes link elements that are stylesheet with precedence, setting the compatibility target.
forces.quote: Because React 19 appears to dedupe only link elements that are stylesheet + precedence
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The fix must not change public API or types.
forces.quote: No public API or type changes.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Changes must be limited to internal JSX runtime link hoisting/dedupe behavior and tests.
forces.quote: Changes are limited to internal JSX runtime `link` hoisting/dedupe behavior and tests.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Previously-deduped link variants (canonical, alternate, preload, icon, preconnect, etc.) are now preserved in output.
consequences.quote: As shown below, the list of links with issues reported in the issue will also remain without being deduplicated.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: After the review-requested refactor, shouldDeDupeByKey's props argument became unused.
consequences.quote: fix: remove unused argument from shouldDeDupeByKey
consequences.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Extend link dedupe keys to ['href', 'rel', 'hreflang'] with AND semantics
alternatives.rejection_reason: Rejected in favor of aligning with React 19's narrower model, which dedupes only stylesheet links with precedence, rather than broadening the multi-key dedupe semantics as the issue proposed.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: It fixes over-deduplication by href (for canonical / alternate / preload / icon / preconnect, etc.) and narrows dedupe to stylesheet links with precedence only.
alternatives.author: usualoma

decisions.title: Add classNameSlug option to createCssContext for customizing generated CSS class names
decisions.summary: The default CSS helper always generates cryptic hash-based class names like css-2458908649, which hurts readability and debugging. Add an optional classNameSlug function to createCssContext that receives (hash, label, styleString) and returns a custom class name.

context.claim: The default CSS helper always generates cryptic hash-based class names like css-2458908649, which hurts readability and debugging.
context.quote:
Currently, when using `css` template literal helper, the generated CSS class looks cryptic, such as `css-2458908649`. 

My proposal is an option to customize it because of readability and debugging (especially for development phase).
context.author: jiri-matejka

context.claim: The feature request cited Linaria's classNameSlug configuration as prior art for the desired API shape.
context.quote: For example, [Linaria](https://linaria.dev/) zero-runtime CSS in JS offers `classNameSlug` in its [configuration](https://github.com/callstack/linaria/blob/master/docs/CONFIGURATION.md).
context.author: jiri-matejka

context.claim: The request is targeted at Hono's built-in css helper for server-side zero-runtime CSS generation, not Emotion/SWC/Babel.
context.quote:
I mean this as an enhancement directly into Hono, specifically the builtin [css helper](https://hono.dev/docs/helpers/css).

Because if I understand the Hono's `css` helper, it is a custom made helper, not Emotion JS.

Which I did not mention, is that I am talking about server side generation (zero runtime) of CSS.
context.author: jiri-matejka

decision.claim: Add an optional classNameSlug function to createCssContext that receives (hash, label, styleString) and returns a custom class name.
decision.quote: Adds an optional `classNameSlug` function to `createCssContext` that lets users customize generated CSS class names instead of the default `css-1234567890` format.
decision.author: flow-pie

decision.claim: The direction of the PR was endorsed by the maintainer early in review.
decision.quote:
@flow-pie Thank you for the PR!

@usualoma, can you review this? I think the direction of it is good!
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: Final design also includes an onInvalidSlug callback (defaulting to console.warn) plus strict validation instead of silent sanitization.
decision.quote: Instead, how about adding a fallback method like `onInvalidSlug`? It's more Hono-way.
decision.author: yusukebe

decision.claim: The feature was approved and merged as-is, released as a patch version.
decision.quote: I'll merge this PR immediately. This change is a new feature, but slight, so I will release a patch version with this feature.
decision.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: classNameSlug must be propagated through all CSS primitives (cssCommon, keyframesCommon, viewTransitionCommon) and both SSR and DOM-side createCssContext for consistency.
forces.quote:
1. **DOM-side `createCssContext`** (`src/jsx/dom/css.ts`) is not updated to support `classNameSlug`.
2. **`keyframesCommon`** and **`viewTransitionCommon`** in `src/helper/css/common.ts` don't propagate `classNameSlug`.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: The user-facing API should not require users to call .trim() themselves; normalization should happen on the framework side.
forces.quote: Is it best to impose user `trim()` by themselves? Or should you implement it on the framework side?
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: A malicious or careless classNameSlug return value could inject CSS and break the generated class attribute, so returned values must be constrained.
forces.quote: Shouldn't you escape the class name? For instance, if someout put `bad name{color:red}` the HTML attribubte will be `class="bad name{color:red}"`. If so, the CSS name will be broken(?).
forces.author: yusukebe

forces.claim: Performance must not be compromised on the default path when classNameSlug is not used; the internally generated hash is considered safe and should not be re-checked.
forces.quote: First, as a general rule, I don't want to compromise performance in cases where `ClassNameSlug` isn't used. I consider the hash generated internally to be safe, so I don't want to perform any checks.
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Validation must enforce valid CSS identifier syntax (reject digit-leading names like 1hero) and reject CSS-wide reserved keywords for @keyframes names.
forces.quote:
- `validateClassName`: Use `^-?[_a-zA-Z][_a-zA-Z0-9-]*$` instead of `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]` to enforce valid CSS identifier syntax (e.g. reject names starting with a digit like `1hero`)
- `validateKeyframeName`: Additionally reject [CSS-wide reserved keywords](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/custom-ident#description) (`none`, `inherit`, `initial`, `unset`, `default`, `revert`, `revert-layer`) which are invalid as `@keyframes` names
forces.author: usualoma

forces.claim: Distinct user inputs differing only by special characters must not collapse to the same class name (e.g. class-name-! vs class-name-?).
forces.quote: Also, in this context (and generally in many cases), I don't think `sanitize` is appropriate. This is because problems would arise if user-generated `class-name-!` and `class-name-?` were treated as identical by `sanitize`.
forces.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Invalid slugs silently fall back to the hash, but an onInvalidSlug callback (defaulting to console.warn) notifies the user.
consequences.quote:
- Add optional onInvalidSlug callback to createCssContext for custom
  error handling (defaults to console.warn).
consequences.author: flow-pie

consequences.claim: Labels are pre-normalized (trim + whitespace-to-hyphen) before being passed to classNameSlug, so users don't need to call .trim() themselves.
consequences.quote: - Pre-normalize labels (trim + hyphenate) before passing to classNameSlug.
consequences.author: flow-pie

consequences.claim: Unicode/multibyte characters are not permitted in slugs even though CSS technically supports them, since there is no imagined user need.
consequences.quote: Thanks for pointing that out. I know it’s possible to use multibyte characters in class names, but I just can’t imagine any users deliberately trying to use them in this context.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: createCssContext remains marked experimental, so the API may still change.
consequences.quote:
* `createCssContext` is an experimental feature.
 * The API might be changed.
consequences.author: usualoma

consequences.claim: Default behavior is preserved: when classNameSlug is not provided, the existing hash-based class name is emitted with zero added overhead.
consequences.quote: - Ensure the default path remains zero-overhead when classNameSlug is unused.
consequences.author: flow-pie

alternatives.name: Emit no warning at all when classNameSlug returns an invalid value (treat it purely as user responsibility)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Considered because invalid slugs are user error, but rejected in favor of a configurable onInvalidSlug callback so developers are not left guessing why custom names aren't applied.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Since this is essentially the user’s responsibility, I don’t think we need to display anything, but one option might be to use `console.warn()` regardless of the environment (development/production).
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Permissive validation regex [a-zA-Z0-9_-] for slug characters
alternatives.rejection_reason: Replaced with stricter ^-?[_a-zA-Z][_a-zA-Z0-9-]*$ to enforce valid CSS identifier syntax and reject digit-leading names like '1hero'.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: - `validateClassName`: Use `^-?[_a-zA-Z][_a-zA-Z0-9-]*$` instead of `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]` to enforce valid CSS identifier syntax (e.g. reject names starting with a digit like `1hero`)
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Allow Unicode/multibyte characters in slugs (since CSS permits them)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Multibyte-character slugs were acknowledged as technically valid CSS but rejected because no realistic user need was imagined for this context.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Thanks for pointing that out. I know it’s possible to use multibyte characters in class names, but I just can’t imagine any users deliberately trying to use them in this context.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Validate* helpers that return string | undefined (name itself)
alternatives.rejection_reason: Since slug generation and validation are separate concerns, the validators were simplified to return a boolean (isValidClassName / isValidKeyframeName) instead of returning the string or undefined.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Now that slug generation and validation are separate, `validate*` functions just return `boolean` instead of the string itself
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Name the third parameter of ClassNameSlug 'css'
alternatives.rejection_reason: The parameter name 'css' could be confused with the `css` tagged template function; renamed to 'styleString' for clarity.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: **`ClassNameSlug` type** (minor, just my opinion): The third parameter name `css` could be confused with the `css` tagged template function. I'd suggest renaming it to `styleString` for clarity.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Place the ClassNameSlug type next to the goober attribution comment
alternatives.rejection_reason: Its placement made it look like part of the goober-derived code; moved near other type exports (around CssVariableType) for clarity.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: **`ClassNameSlug` type placement**: It's currently placed right below the goober attribution comment, which makes it look like it's part of the goober-derived code. Moving it near the other type exports (around `CssVariableType`) would be clearer.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Sanitize classNameSlug output by stripping unsafe characters (e.g. trim + collapse whitespace + strip to [a-zA-Z0-9_-])
alternatives.rejection_reason: Sanitization would collapse semantically distinct user inputs (e.g. 'class-name-!' and 'class-name-?') into the same class name, causing collisions. Rejected in favor of strict validation with fallback to hash.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Also, in this context (and generally in many cases), I don't think `sanitize` is appropriate. This is because problems would arise if user-generated `class-name-!` and `class-name-?` were treated as identical by `sanitize`.
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: HTML-escape the class name rather than constrain the character set
alternatives.rejection_reason: Escaping was discussed but a character-set restriction ([a-zA-Z0-9_-] initially, later tightened to a valid-CSS-identifier regex) was judged best; escaping was not adopted.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: Should we escape it, or perhaps simply restrict it to something like [a-zA-Z0-9_-]?
alternatives.author: usualoma

alternatives.name: Dev-only warning gated behind process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' for invalid slugs
alternatives.rejection_reason: Hono avoids depending on process.env because it varies by runtime; an environment-variable-gated warning was rejected in favor of a user-configurable onInvalidSlug callback.
alternatives.rejection_reason_quoted: I don't prefer to use an environment variable. In the `hono` code, we don't use it so many times because it depends on the runtime. Instead, how about adding a fallback method like `onInvalidSlug`? It's more Hono-way.
alternatives.author: yusukebe
